speech_text: The crux of this debate is simple: do we want a world where people are enslaved by perfect recollection, or one where the flaws in our memory allow us to move forward? On happy memories, the Government tells you the guilt of forgetting is painful, but Opposition shows you that imperfection is what gives meaning. When you forget the small details, what remains is the feeling, and nostalgia fills those gaps with warmth, not cold facts. Their world removes that magic and replaces it with flat recordings that lose value the more you replay them, creating diminishing marginal utility. On trauma, Government claims therapy and courts benefit, but therapy already works by controlled recollection, not by forcing perfect video that retraumatizes victims. In courts, reliving your worst night frame by frame is more harmful than healing, and “opt-in” is meaningless when survivors can’t anticipate long-term harms. On surveillance, their claim that states won’t abuse this tech is absurd—governments already pressure citizens into adopting invasive systems, and once memories can be exported, minorities, protesters, and vulnerable groups are the first to suffer. When weighing, our side shows that joy comes from imperfect memory, forgetting is a gift in the face of trauma, and privacy protects millions every day, whereas their benefits are niche and fragile. At the end of the day, their world is one where people can never escape the past, while ours is one where people live, forget, and move forward. Proud to oppose.
judge_note: Clear contextualization of misuse potentials. Decent argumentation on ethical dilemmas. Structuring effective with fresh angles. Delivery steady;

motion: This house will stop building trade relations with countries that have poor human rights record.
speech_text: Ladies and gentlemen, honorable adjudicators, today this House believes that we should suspend trade relations with countries that have poor human rights records. At the heart of this motion is a simple but profound question: do we prioritize short-term economic gains, or do we stand firmly for the dignity and humanity of people everywhere? Government says that trade is not neutral—it legitimizes regimes, empowers elites, and sustains the very abuses we claim to condemn. Let me be clear from the outset: this debate is not about punishing ordinary citizens. It is about holding governments and elites accountable for systematic violations such as forced labor, suppression of free speech, discrimination against minorities, and extrajudicial killings. If we continue to trade with these states, we send the message that human rights are negotiable, that profit is more important than principle. That is unacceptable. I will advance two main arguments in my opening speech. First, the principle: why trading with abusive states undermines universal human rights. Second, the practical: why economic leverage through trade suspension is one of the few effective tools the international community possesses. On principle, human rights are universal and indivisible. When we trade freely with abusers, we implicitly condone their violations. It is hypocritical for democratic nations to speak of freedom while simultaneously fueling regimes that silence and exploit their people. Ending trade ties affirms that rights come before riches, that no government can buy impunity with cheap exports or lucrative markets. On practicality, trade is one of the strongest bargaining chips in diplomacy. Authoritarian leaders may ignore speeches at the UN, but they cannot ignore the collapse of revenue when major trade partners withdraw. Suspending trade creates both internal and external pressure: internal, because citizens and local businesses will demand reform once prosperity is jeopardized; external, because states seeking legitimacy will need to comply with international standards to regain access to global markets. Far from isolating ordinary people, this strategy forces regimes to address structural abuses or face long-term decline. In conclusion, we in Government believe that human rights must not be treated as an optional add-on to commerce. Trade is a privilege, not a right. If states refuse to respect the basic dignity of their citizens, then they do not deserve the benefits of international economic cooperation. For all these reasons, we are proud to propose.
judge_note: Solid counter-case with effective contextualization of economic interdependence. Good argumentation defending trade's neutral role and citizen harms. Clear structuring, though examples may overextend. Delivery clear with minor hesitations;

motion: This House believes that the reimposition of Death Penalty is within the interests of Businesses in the Philippines.
speech_text: In order to understand where the death penalty is the best solution to alleviate the problems in the country, we have to look at two things. Firstly, why there is a public clamor for the death penalty and secondly, why this public clamor necessarily means that the death penalty will be more effective than it was in the past. Firstly, the reason that there is a public clamor for death penalty is because the citizens no longer trust the state, they no longer trust the current justice system and they no longer believe that the police for example is able to protect them and is able to uphold their rights. How does the death penalty change this perception? This changes because the people are right now calling for the death penalty. They have already elected President of Derridae to represent them at the highest office in the country based on his platform against drugs and the re-institution of the death penalty. The reason why this is important is because once the administration decides to re-institutionalize the death penalty, this goes to show that the administration is willing to listen to the people. It shows that the state is once again reconnecting with the populace that has long felt abandoned and has long felt left behind. Secondly, the reason why this public clamor will make the death penalty a better system now is because of this public clamor you can see that people are now concerned as to how this process will take place. They are concerned as to how the death penalty will be re-instituted and how the justice system will be reformed. This means that there will be a greater level of accountability coming from the negative side when we tell you that the death penalty is going to be re-instituted. These people are not going to simply dismiss it as another useless attempt of the government to change an already broken system but they will see it as the government taking what they believe to be the best solution and implementing it on a nationwide scale. But now, let's talk about why for businesses this is going to be the best solution. Well, coming from the opposition side, they have completely discussed this misty idea of the value of the appearance of safety. We have argued coming from the government side that the value of safety is always going to be of paramount importance when it comes to securing small businesses especially. Because when you talk about small businesses, these are usually located in rural areas with low police presence and with low population concentration. Meaning that in order for people to want to start businesses there to begin, they have to feel secure, they have to feel that their businesses are not immediately going to be shut down because of an ongoing drug problem within their area. That is what the death penalty sense. It sends a message that the government is taking hard stands against crime and that this hard stand is going to translate into people feeling safe, into people feeling like their businesses are going to be able to flourish because the state is there to protect them, the state is there to ensure that their rights are upheld and that they are able to prosper. Because at the end of the day, we have to see that the death penalty might not be in a more degree area, but we have to understand that its impact on people is something that is definite. It's impact on how people perceive themselves, perceive their society and perceive their state is something that cannot be discounted. Even if we say that it is not necessarily lower the crime rate, it at least gives people that hope that crime is being combated, that crime is being fixed and it gives them that hope to see a better future for themselves, to see a better future for their country, to see a better future for them where they grow as people and that will inter-translate into people becoming more confident in their businesses and with those businesses growing to become even better.

motion: This House believes that it is in the interest of multinational corporations to invest only in countries with good human rights records.
speech_text: We don't deny drugs may be a problem in our societies. However, re-institutionizing the death penalty is the wrong way to go. They claim that this works as an effective deterrent to ensure that we scare individuals and to ensure that they are set off drugs. However, we have to understand that the terms of death penalty only work when individuals are sure that they actually will be prosecuted, that they will receive the death penalty. But if we look at the reality that exists in the Philippines today, this isn't the case. Our rate of prosecution is so low that individuals, even if they are in jail, are highly unlikely to face trials. And even if they are, it will take months and years to come. And even in the instance that they do face trial, if we look at the rate of which we actually execute death penalty, we have a 71% judicial error rate. That means even if we implement the death penalty, we are unlikely to implement it effectively enough to serve as the deterrent that they want. But let's say it does serve as a deterrent. Why do we think it's still bad? Because even if it is a deterrent, all we're doing is replacing one type of fear, which is the fear of drugs, with another type of fear. The fear that the police are going to come and they're going to essentially bring you to jail and have the death penalty upon you. Because if we look at it today, operations like Operation Tokang, or the type of tribe that stops individuals from committing drugs, usually do so out of simple accusations that people are drug dealers. And therefore, individuals on the ground, rural individuals, who might not have access to things like lawyers or natural protective mechanisms, are still going to be afraid of going out, are still going to be afraid, essentially because if the police catch them, if someone wrongfully accuses them of doing drugs, they are just as dead as the individuals who are guilty. And that's not the world we want to live in. It's just why, essentially, we believe that the redistitution of the death penalty harms Philippine businesses, whether they are small- to medium enterprises or large enterprises, and we would rather they simply improve current judicial systems and current police implementation to ensure safety. Two reasons why it's not in the interest of businesses. First is essentially because it harms trade. In status quo right now, we are greatly benefited by systems such as the general system of preference, which allows us to ensure that codes 6,274 goods can enter duty-free in the areas like the European Union, for example. And a lot of these codes are focused on the agricultural sector, things like coconut oil, or coconut water, or coconut everything. The point is that this is a $12.8 billion trade that a lot of the times directly affects. The businesses and the agricultural sector of individuals on the ground. The problem is, these types of benefits that we are currently enjoying today are highly dependent on our ability to keep our human rights records straight, our ability to ensure that we adhere to the treaties and the second option protocol that we as the Philippines are a signatory to. The harm, if ever, we do re-institution-like death penalties. I.S. 1, we essentially risk losing the benefit state, our GSE status, and this essentially trickles down to over 200,000 agricultural jobs being lost. These are people in the rural areas, people they wanted to be there to protect, now being unemployed, and now being essentially jobless and not having any kind of option in one to turn to. We think that being economically stable is one of the steps to ensure that you know what, you don't fall into trouble, that you are able to have a stable life. But when you rip the rug out from under these individuals, all of the more we essentially lead them towards that downward spiral. That's why if we want to ensure that we spend this state, we don't want to re-institution-like the death penalty. But the second reason is essentially because it ruins investor confidence in the Philippines. The second option protocol in the ITCPR aims for the abolishment of death penalty and it's not something you can simply just withdraw from the will. That's why we think it's an institutional reflection of what the government's unwillingness to keep its commitments but think it's all the more unwillingness to simply backtrack from any of the treaties or its promises that are out of the way. It is a message that you cannot trust the words of the Philippine government when it is just willing to pull out some things that it has cited in that too. And this is essentially bad because the Philippine business allows for the time benefits from collaborations and from partnerships with foreign businesses. It benefits from partnerships with foreign institutions. However, if these foreign institutions do not feel safe and do not think that they can trust the government when they enter into business, they are unlikely to do so. This essentially lessens our opportunity to expand, to grow our businesses and to ensure that the Philippine economy does grow. Just why, for all these reasons, we'd rather not play institutionalized the death penalty. Thank you.
judge_note: Clear contextualization of corporate incentives tied to human rights. Good argumentation on reputational gains and risk mitigation. Nice structuring with defined burdens and logical flow. Confident delivery with engaging setup.

motion: This House believes that it is in the interest of multinational corporations to invest only in countries with good human rights records.
speech_text: Ladies and gentlemen, MNCs only really have two interests, money and markets. In achieving these two, MNCs have invested spectacular amounts of capital in newer technologies and cheaper products that are now seen globally. These things are necessary for the advancement of countries in poor human rights records and poor economic development. We would posit then that in letting MNCs pursue profit margins continuously without threats or pullouts, we can better protect the marginalized. So what do we envision on the Opposition side? First we think it is helpful and even necessary for MNCs to continuously invest in countries with poor human rights records. Secondly, who are these countries? Because we didn't get a clear picture from the Opposition side. Generally these are countries who score low in freedom indices by renowned human rights institutes like the Freedom House and Plato Institute. These are countries with weak governments and repressed populations that are common in sub-Saharan Africa, South Asia and the Middle East. But lastly what do we mean by invest? We mean not only investing in your supply chains in those countries but also investing in the new consumer markets, the people they can sell them to. A brief response before I go to my case, we think only with continuous engagement by these MNCs can we actually effect change in these states. What does this mean? Only when MNCs continuously grow and continuously expand their markets and productions in these countries can people on the ground demand a greater share of this prosperity. That's why when MNCs started entering the Chinese market in the 1990s, we had an increase in the number of protest incidents by Chinese workers from the 1990s, numbering from only the hundreds to the tenth of thousands today. People want their money, people want their wages and you cannot get that if people pull out. Conversely, if these MNCs pull out under their side then it only means that these states will revert back to heavy-handed economic policies that threaten prosperity. We're talking about state-owned enterprises under control of the cronies of the dictator. We're talking about import tariffs and protections that only start people on the ground. Let's go to the things I will discuss. First thing I will discuss money. Why do we think that MNCs must expand into worse-off countries to remain productive? At some point, MNCs must look outside their home countries due to market saturation because even if you are rich or middle class, you will only buy so many cars or so many shoes and there's only so much land and so much natural resources for these MNCs to build new factories and new products. We think countries, unfortunately countries with poor human rights records pose great opportunities for these MNCs. When we have a corrupt and inefficient government that has inefficient state-owned enterprises, it means there are many natural resources that are not yet used or yet untapped as of, untapped by these countries. We are talking about low income and repressed populations. We are talking about cheap labor. Our consumers who do not know the fruits of labor from multinational corporations. So what we think is entering these countries by MNCs not only gives us more productivity for these MNCs but prosperity to the countries. With MNCs now, local workers now have a chance to earn higher wages in marginally better conditions than if they worked in a state-owned factory. Now, local companies can see partnerships, more honest and lucrative contracts with MNCs rather than having to go through corrupt bidding processes or having to get substituted by a corrupt state-owned enterprise. Now, people on the ground can get cheap and actually better goods rather than the ones that we have to get through, through bad and through high import taxes. A case study here is the Philippines. If you'll notice so many products here are of value size that there is no parallel in the world like those kinds of products because MNCs have had to innovate to be able to compensate for the low incomes and low opportunities of the Filipinos. The conclusion here is that if you allow MNCs to come in and profit off of us, we will be able to profit off of their better products and their better jobs. Secondly, unmarkets. Why do you think that MNCs entry, continuous entry invites more involvement in the human rights situation? We think protecting free trade is a priority for countries because no one wants their business or their businessmen to be harmed no matter where they are in the globe. That's why since the 1990s, the United States and the European Union have been incorporating human rights languages into their free trade agreements. The example here would be the generalized scheme of preferences held by the EU which commits countries who want to trade with the EU to international conventions on human and labor rights. We think the withdrawal of benefits to corrupt governments is the stick that these free trade agreements want to have. But the carrot is the MNCs and their continuous investment in the people and the markets in these countries with poor human rights records. The capital and the productivity is what governments in these places want. The opportunities and wages are what the people want. And if you threaten to take them away on their side, you will not get reform. You will not get changes. You will not get pushed back by the workers on the ground. We think it is through the drive of MNCs to expand the new markets. Can markets actualize themselves? Can markets demand better rights? And this is what we stand for on the opposition side. Thank you.
judge_note: Solid counter-case with effective contextualization of profit-driven motives. Good argumentation defending cost efficiencies in diverse markets. Clear structuring, though examples slightly extend flow. Delivery clear with minor hesitations;

motion: This House believes that it is in the interest of multinational corporations to invest only in countries with good human rights records.
speech_text: We agree that for the longest time, the interests of MNCs have always been profit-driven. But if you know this, ladies and gentlemen, a growing consensus is that in status quo, is that in order for us to have real change and sustainable change in many parts of the country, we need to include it in a crucial actor, which is the private actor, ladies and gentlemen. And with the NMC is the perfect actor for which we reject their corporate premise that MNCs should only be concerned and are only concerned with profit-driven. If anything, we say that it's important for them to take part because of their moral responsibility, but more than that as well, we also think that also allows them to maximize profit as well. This is precisely the case that I will forward to you in my case. So the first one, I will forward to you two things. The first one is that why do we think that MNCs, when they invest, for instance, or put up businesses, they become complicit to the human rights violations? The first one, we think that when MNCs promote, invest, and put up businesses in countries where there are poor human rights violations or poor human rights records, they legitimize and benefit all of the abuse. How is this true? Firstly, on benefit, we think that when MNCs promote or when MNCs, for instance, invest in countries with no-up or poor human rights record, this means that they actually, there's a tendency for them to take advantage of poor people's conditions, for instance, like sweatshops, where people are overworked and underpaid. We think, ladies and gentlemen, this is a direct response to the government's up-to-side oppositions case that when you have MNCs on the ground, suddenly people in the poor margins will be able to alleviate themselves. We think that this is not necessarily true. But the second thing is this as well. We think that when MNCs also go in low-up in countries with poor human rights record, there's also a tendency for them to take advantage of the poor environmental laws there. This means that there's a tendency for them to actually overuse the resources. What does this tell us? It means that to a certain extent, ladies and gentlemen, these MNCs have for the longest time been able to benefit from human rights violations. We think it's high time for us to deviate from that, and we think this is the perfect chance. The second thing is this. We think that when MNCs invest in countries with poor human rights record, they also legitimize the abuse. Why is this true? Because not only are they able to benefit and actually maximize the abuse, the taxes that they pay to the state ladies and gentlemen will not necessarily go to far-to-market routes that are used for industries to alleviate the poor, but will actually be used by the state, the human rights violator, to actually abuse its people as well. What does this tell us? It means that the tax coming from these MNCs is actually used by states in order to abuse their citizens. The MNC therefore becomes complicit to the action of the state. Why is this not, why is this something that we are against coming from side government? We think that firstly, it's a moral responsibility coming from them, primarily because we have to recognize that we shouldn't just treat MNCs as mere robots that necessarily want to maximize profit. We should think of them as actors as well that can affect change in status quo. But more than that as well, we think that there's also a benefit in their side as well. So this is not just about them having that moral responsibility. It's also about their reputation as well. Because if you notice in status quo, in so many instances, the global media has become so powerful already. To a point where in the big and easy to report, human rights violations have been in status quo. Even in the instance where in Pepsi, for instance, promoted the log form of social justice, there was a large backlash against it. We think that when companies become complicit to human rights violations, there's a larger backlash than that. That's why for instance, if you remember Shell, had its reputation destroyed at the point in time, it actually harmed the indigenous rights of people in Africa. In comparison to Chevron, what necessarily promotes ethical oil. This is vindicatively disinjettlement. That competition is not just about our prices as well, but it's also about trying to maximize your reputation. Because that reputation allows you to have more consumers and have better confidence among the states. And for all of those reasons, we're proud of the proposed. Thanks.

judge_note: Clear weighing of reputational and financial benefits with solid rebuttals. Good argumentation reinforcing stakeholder trust. Structuring coherent but less dynamic. Delivery solid;

motion: This House believes that the Philippines should prioritize its foreign trade relations over its war on drugs.'
speech_text: In buying rifles for his drug war, Rodrigo Deterte as neglected to buy rice for his people. He has been obsessed with striking fear into the hearts of criminals, but fear cannot fill the stomachs of the poor. It is high time that the Philippine government got his priorities straight. It is time we answered to both national and international commitments to protecting human rights or everyone will pay. What do we envision on side government? Essentially, what we would want the Philippine government to do is to prioritize fixing his foreign trade relations over continuing the war on drugs. What do we mean? Firstly, we would invite Rodrigo Deterte to use his remaining political capital to commit to an explicit timetable of fixing foreign trade agreements with specific parties. In this debate, we would envision that he uses that capital to have a formal agreement with the European Union to have a timetable to reform the HR situation. That within five to ten years, they will go back to committing to the same international conventions they agreed to when they signed on to the generalized scheme of preferences. Secondly, we would posit that he would want to cut down on the drug war because it is no longer his proper priority. We are looking at a reduction of top hand. We are looking at a reduction of watchness. We are looking at a reduction of killings or maybe even total abandonment. Only when he has fixed these commitments could he possibly entertain going back to the drug war, but we think with the effects of recommitting the foreign trade relations, we highly doubt that will happen. Of course, opposition would agree that the war on drugs at his aid as it is is currently unacceptable, but since they will have to defend the war on drugs as a higher priority, they must defend the scenario without an explicit agreement to fixing the human rights conditions. They have to defend the scenario where reforms move slower and people die more quickly. The first thing I will talk about then in my speech is why prioritizing the war on drugs will lead to the same conditions that Deterte sought to eradicate. We think that the war on drugs has been terrible for the business community. For foreign investors, the murder of the Hangein executive, Ji-Hik-Joo, in the police headquarters in Cap Kramin, and the bundled investigations of the government after tells them many things. They are not safe from crime and murder. The police cannot be trusted with their protection and the government is willing to protect, to tolerate this ineptitude. For local businesses, the war on drugs means that more family members will die and so will the workers. Generally, it is unsafe to do business. Generally, it is unsafe to live in the Philippines. We think the continued priority of the war on drugs is not conducive to foreign trade relations, which are premised on stable and safe markets, which are premised on transparent and accountable governments. We think the resulting reductions on foreign trade relations, like the possible reintroduction of tariffs, the possible reduction of allowable Filipino products to be exported to other countries, for unions like the European Union, will have many costs if we continue to prioritize the war on drugs. This means decreased profits for whatever remaining exports we have. This means reduced goods for our consumers, or even more expensive goods for our consumers, and generally fewer opportunities abroad for our own citizens. This decreased prosperity can lead to conditions of poverty in one. The same conditions that encourage the gravity and criminality that the war on drugs sought to eradicate. The conclusion is simple. If you lose pre-trade, you will lose both pre-trade and the war on drugs. The second thing I want to talk about, why is prioritizing pre-trade relations going to ease the government's burden in fighting drugs? Because we do agree the drug problem is problematic to the country. We think in prioritizing pre-trade relations, we are allowed to create good business conditions and improve the human rights situation. We are talking about improving the security apparatus of the state, meaning trust the policemen and military men that we can trust to protect us. We are talking about transparency and bureaucracy, not only in the investigations that policemen can have, but also investigations when goods go missing in ports or tariffs or not calculated property by the BIR. We are also talking about improving competition in business, which is usually an indicator of increased opportunities for the less fortunate or even the reform criminal who wants to make a living. Generally, in being able to prove these conditions to foreign investors and re-establishing our commitments to foreign trade relations, this can generally result in greater prosperity for the country. So let's look at different scenarios of what retriever Duterte might choose to do once he reappears in foreign trade relations, once he reappears his commitment to human rights. In the best case, Duterte and the government will now be so committed to foreign trade relations that he will no longer commit to the punitive actions of his war on drugs. He might just focus on rehabilitation or rebuilding the lives of the people on the ground. But in the worst case, if Duterte has the gall to continue the war on drugs, then he will be easily restrained by the more prosperous population of the Willoughby's because people will no longer be hungry enough to think that bullets can easily fill their stomach as bread. Ladies and gentlemen, we think prosperity is the key to peace. That is what we stand for on government side. You're proud of a post.
judge_note: Weak argumentation. Good contextualization. Decent structure and use of examples. Monotone delivery.

motion: This House believes that the Philippines should prioritize its foreign trade relations over its war on drugs.'
speech_text: Well, I'm pretty sure this debate is in the debate about trade relations versus extra-judicial killings because that's not something debaters rationally should be allowed to defend or forced to defend for that matter. What we think of the debate is there for about is the trends to which the war on drugs is turning towards. That means four things. One is that we are actually trying to crack down on trying to stop the EJK, in fact still being able to crack down on drug-related violence. Note how we agree with affirmative side that Operation TohKam, for example, was at first bottom hold, then it was recommissioned but with a tighter leash. That's to say, the detainee has acknowledged that there has been harms that the PNP has been doing but it continues the war and is continuing to make those necessarily concessions that human rights to an extent is something he also prioritizes. The second thing that we think that the war on drugs is moving towards is obviously that it's threatening the police. And we think that in our model, we think that we can do things like increasing the amount of drugs that we've got, training better for disarming the criminals instead of shooting them down, and better intelligence and the way that we apply that intelligence. What about our rights? Ultimately, the war on drugs has two goals. One is that they want to spot the supply chain and that they want to curb the addiction. And this means that sometimes to an extent it might kill people in the process of trying to detain them. We think that this war is something that's legitimate for a multitude of reasons. The first thing I want to talk about is why foreign trade isn't really as government thinks it is. It isn't just all or nothing. Three reasons. One, we tell you that if you lose one training partner, it's not as if you won't have any other training partner. Because naturally the world is full of other countries and other people can trade with other people. But note how Duterte has been so willing to shift from the West to the East to the West and back. If the world is so globalized that government says it's something we have to keep as well, that means that it's also equally as hard to try to untie each other from other countries. But the third thing that we want to posit is that if the West does cut off all trade with the Philippines, the war will not stop. If it's all cut off, we tell you that the West does it with invest insights. If government wants our position signed to defend not having a timetable on trade relations, that's okay. But let's answer it. Who is the Philippines? We see now that the Philippines is not only the government, but it's precisely the people on the ground. Meaning, compared to foreign trade relations, the rural masses who drove Duterte and a majority seat to victory, jobs are an issue closer to their homes. So if it's a comparison about not having food to eat versus the job problem being eradicated, know how people have already made their decision. Comparatively, trade only benefits the upper class. It then trenches the oligarchs and the rich. And know how this is a system that 16 million Filipinos rejected in the 2016 election because the trickle-down effect is something that seems to never have reached the poor thought. Know how, therefore, lastly, that we think that we can't forego foreign trade relations because the drug war is directly a mandate at the interests of the people. If this debate is about what the Philippines wants, we think it's about what the people wants. The premise to this argument is that elections are a parameter for societal interests and priorities. So if 16 million people voted for Duterte and his platform of drug eradication, even if it doesn't mention feeding them, but we think it does mention feeding them, but even if the extent that it doesn't talk about trade relations, people have already made up their minds. And think that it's not so easy for government side to say we can just shift the political will of the people is clearly with over 76% of an approval rating, clearly that's not going to be something we're going to stop anytime soon. This argument is just to say that people feel and perceive this as a primary issue compared to the issue of feeding themselves or having trade that has only benefited the rich and hasn't trickled down to the farmers in the rural areas who have the drug problem or possession tax.

motion: This House believes that the Philippines should prioritize its foreign trade relations over its war on drugs.'
speech_text: We need more than self-serving government peers at this turbulent junction. The crux of the war on drugs is prohibition and a vast number of researchers have pointed to a well-known conclusion that it does not curb the matter. It only restricts it in one area, which is the government side, and allows other criminal entrepreneurs to fight for this well-sought good, which obviously would have both been through a crunchy play in so many issues, exacerbated now by the current form of a war on drugs. Even the first speaker of opposition side told us that there is a political motive to offer for the therapy to actually double his efforts in competing war on drugs in its current form, because it will differentiate itself from as a strongman, violently tackling drugs as opposed to his predecessors, for example, who he claims for the person-made situation. Even if Duterte says that we're not actually imposing, but Philippines has already entered these international commitments and it's our responsibility to abide by those. Now, second, an opposition's model, we're looking at a scenario where they are okay with aligning with countries who openly back for Duterte's war on drugs. This is again harmful, because as we see now, this has resulted in a misallocation of resources to the detriment of poverty reduction programs and other programs related to people's basic needs, from sanitation to education to healthcare, which are primary issues that have been chopped to the back of our priorities because of the obsession on this war on drugs in its current form. But even if, however, on our side, if we prioritize foreign trade relations, there is more potential for us to actually improve the social economic conditions of the people, to include inclusiveness in our agenda, to include the boosting of the neglected sectors, which we can actually capitalize on to ensure that people are well educated, people can make better decisions in the next election, for example, or people can make decisions that are not related or well-being at the end of the day. Now, on our side, we can get a short timeframe of prioritization for government so that we're not going to leave this to chance, we're not going to leave this to the mercy of the switch of the mind of our leader. Let me tell you that this is the right time for us to actually prioritize foreign trade relations, to actually solve the war on drugs, which on the opposition's side, leads to a bit of the other way around. So again, we're very proud to prioritize foreign trade relations, and I'm going to let Asian-Chadon, the war on drugs, use career for it.
judge_note: Decent argumentation. Sufficient analysis. Good delivery despite minor pauses and errors, degrades as the speech progresses.

Are our people within the communities that we never talked about actually giving the life that we want? We think that though, having looked at this at the lens of the people in Maranta or in Booting in the southern part of the Philippines, we think that in this course or in this particular cities, we go to school with the richest children in this particular city only the families are actually rich in these countries. But when you ask them actually, what is the source of the livelihoods of their parents, they don't know because they're not supposed to know. They're not supposed to talk about these kinds of things because they have been living their lives privilege enough to actually benefit from illegal drug trades. motion: This House believes that the Philippines should prioritize its foreign trade relations over its war on drugs.'
speech_text: These are gentlemen, what is making is always about tradeoffs. We cannot create a policy that caters to what everyone wants and what everyone needs. However, we have to understand that Philippine policy has to be geared towards the Filipino people. We should not make our policies based on what foreign powers want and what our people need. So now we take a look at what both models offer. Coming from the government side, they offer us a best-case model wherein we have strong trade relations and we have strong partnerships with many foreign powers. They claim that this will automatically trickle down to the people who feel let out. However, they fail to address the primary problem that propelled the territory of power, which is that people feel unsafe in their homes, they feel unsafe in their communities because of the rampant drug problem. Coming from the opposition side, however, we present to you, even in our worst-case model, where in some of our trade relations do go under, they do fall apart, however, they will always exist. Even if, for example, the West completely falls out from the Philippines, there will be other countries willing to trade with us, there will be other powers who are going to provide what the West used to provide. However, the difference coming from our side is that even if some of our trade relations disappear, we still address the basic problem that Filipinos face on a daily basis, which is the feeling of being unsafe and the feeling of being let out. Why does our side promote both good trade relations and a war on drugs? Why do these people not necessarily have to clash? This is because the goal of the war on drugs is simple. It's trying to cut off the supply and lessen the demand for drugs. This can happen without necessarily affecting foreign trade relations. Because foreign trade relations exist, even between countries who have very different approaches to how they address specific problems. This does not mean that having different local policies means that foreign relations will no longer exist. It simply means that they will have to be more careful with dealing with each other. So now, when you take a look at what they said, what they told us that they want to abide with international treaties that the Philippines is a part of, we agree with this, and we say that this also happens on our side. This is what we are trying to tell you, is that we are not going to forward the current form of the drug war, wherein EJ Gays are rampant and police impunity is spread all throughout the country. What's going to happen under our model is that we're going to improve the drug war. We're going to hold the police accountable for their actions. And the first steps towards this have already been taken by the military administration. When they cancel the operation of Tokham in order to make it more accountable and in order to make it less prone to abuse by police officers. Meaning that their entire attention as to why the Philippines should focus on trade relations no longer stands because we already proved to you that improving foreign trade relations can coexist with an ongoing drug war. Because the drug war that we're trying to forward on our side does not necessarily violate international human rights treaties meaning that even our traditional trade partners do not need to feel that they have to separate themselves from us because of bad human rights. But what only happens on our side is that we're combating both the micro level problems that the everyday Filipino feels and the macro level trade relations that both sides want to protect. For all these reasons, the motion falls.
judge_note: Good structure, decent delivery though clearly nervous. Lack of explanation.

motion: THW prefer to be born knowing your fate. - Infoslide: For the purposes of the debate, "fate" refers to all events over the course of one's life, including: the career path you will go into, the exact person you will marry, and the circumstances of your death.
speech_text: The greatest crime ever imposed onto an individual is the absolute responsibility they have over their life. It is the constant pressure to make decisions that you consider are the best in your life, and it is the worry that whether the decisions you've made in your life were truly the ones that were best for you. We think the only way to absolve individuals of this unjust crime they've been imposed is that we're in a world where they're born knowing their own faith. We're so proud to propose. Before I forward two substantive arguments within this speech, first of all, I'd like to characterize what the world looks like on the side of government. One. We would prefer that individuals are born knowing their faith in its entirety. This means that faith is the most important life-changing decisions or moments in an individual experiences. So it can look like things like marriage, death, their career trajectory. But we think at its bare minimum, faith is something that you cannot change. So we think that whilst individuals can still have some autonomy in the minute, inconsequential moments that people experience, we think that these moments don't have more meaning in comparison to the more important things that you experience in your life. Three. This means that they may have some autonomy in their life, but the impact is relatively inconsequential in the bigger picture of things. Lastly, on our burden. But I'd just like to preface this by saying that note that a faith will exist on both sides as implied by the motion. This debate is that when things have already been concretized, should we have individuals have knowledge of their future or should they be left in the dark and we say they shouldn't be born with that knowledge? Two arguments then. motion: TH Supports restrictions on free speech to combat the rise of the right-wing populism.
speech_text: Propositions argument is that right-wing populism is an ideology that can be distilled into five key characteristics. There's such a scientific dissection. Honestly, I think it is very demeaning for their side to say that a quarter of all French people and millions of people all around the world are fundamentally nativistic. We stand against that. What's our stance? One, we will not impose any restrictions on right-wing populist speech. Two, obviously we don't have to stand for threats to an individual's safety like death threats and we will prosecute for acts of violence accordingly. Three, we broadly believe in the value of free speech, even those that are racially tinged. There may come a point where the intent is so malicious and the expressive function of such speech is marginal. In those cases, we will judiciously prosecute for hate speech. The distinction might be porous and when it is unclear, we will err on the side of free speech. Four, proposition's burden is not just to defend current laws restricting free speech but they need to defend restricting the entirety of this political ideology, not just the white noise but also the very legitimate viewpoints that are communicated. We are going forward with three arguments. One, that right-wing populist speech is legitimate and should not be restricted. This deals with the fundamental premise in their case that right-wing populism is something we don't want in society. Two, on the risks of government abuse and three, my second speaker will explain how they harm political discourse. But before that, summary battles. They first told us that a ban was justified because a democracy is about a balance of rights. But this assumes that their policy is the only way to protect minorities. This is not true because ethnic and racial minorities can always vote for the Democratic Party with democratic chess and balances that are in place. But you know who doesn't have an alternative? It's the millions of disenfranchised workers within the ras belt who cannot get another way to express their political views on their political spectrum. We allow for representation, we allow legitimate viewpoints to be expressed. They then say but these are just distractions, it's just white noise. Right-wing populism isn't solution oriented. I think this is honestly very demeaning for to say that half the people in America don't want to vote for actual policies. There are very legitimate policies that can be carried out. For instance, immigration quotas, trade protectionism, etc. It's not just all white noise. They then talk to us about how we may instill fear in minorities. Now, if a person's response to such speech could be fear, but it could also be one of more indignation. And there are many reasons to suggest why more indignation is the more likely response rather than feeling threatened. The reason for this is because the cost of expressing free speech in a status quo is very low. A lot of this speech happens within social media. So these are people who agree with you, etc. There's a reason why when many African Americans listen to Trump's tweet, the way they respond is not a fear or threat, but they respond by retweeting anti-Trump messages and having them shared and affirmed by people who share their beliefs. Before I move on, yes. I just told you my policy as to why, and a certain point where if it's so egregious and the intent is so malicious and there's like very marginal expressive function, we think we will censor those, but we think for the vast majority of right-wing popular speech, it is not like that. That's a majority burden you have to prove. The second argument to us was about discourse. The fundamental premise here is that right-wing populism is harmful. It never provided us an alternative to the problems we see today. My first substantive will now show you why right-wing popular speech is legitimate and should not be restricted. The premise of my first argument, why is free speech valuable? Our identities don't exist in a vacuum. They only gain meaning through expression and interaction with other beliefs. Hence, free speech is a fundamental right that we should not discard from mere convenience. Why is right-wing popular speech particularly vital? Since the 1990s, there has been a radical convergence towards neoliberal centrism in politics. Until recently, most major parties were pro-free trade and globalization. Democrats under Clinton converted Republicans from free trade while labelled in the UK co-opted many aspects of tacharism under black. Why is this consensus harmful? 1. Because a democracy without choice is not a democracy. When there is no party that comes close to representing your interests, then democracy has failed you. What right-wing populism does is to embrace and endorse viewpoints which are perfectly defensible, like the government having a foremost duty to its citizens. So when they tell us it's white noise, we think it's a legitimate standpoint. 2. Because it disenfranchises the millions of workers in the Rust Belt, whose life e-hose have been imperiled by free trade and immigration. They are neglected by a democratic party preoccupied with identity politics and a Republican party that stands for an unbridled free market. These are problems that exist now, they have no solution. Those left behind by free trade are left voiceless. What right-wing popular speech does is to signal that 1. These communities have been disenfranchised and 2. They are electorally determinant. Its upsurgence demands the attention of the political establishment. It has catalyzed the rise in class-based left-leaning politics as Sanders rises in the Democratic Party and Corbyn brings Labour back to its socialist roots. So even if we can agree that right-wing populism itself is a bad idea, it does result in a recalibration of the political spectrum that we think is good for representation. Now we acknowledge that right-wing popular speech may have unsavory elements and this deals with their premise that these unsavory speech is value. 1. But before we move on, yes. Right-wing populists like Trump are known for fake news and lies. Why does that provide a valuable choice for voters if their votes are based on misinformation? 1. I think the fact that they believe in this fake news or if they know it's fake news but still vote for it is still a legitimate form of political expression that they may not care this much about these facts. Therefore, we believe that silence is oppression. We oppose.

motion: TH Supports restrictions on free speech to combat the rise of the right-wing populism.
speech_text: It is problematic for side opposition to stand up and simply deny the racist vitriol that is not only endemic when we look at actual examples of right-wing populists on the ground, the Front National in France, Trump in the United States, Terté in the Philippines, Gert Wilders in the Netherlands, the list goes on. But second of all, when we give you three mechanisms in our first speech as to why this kind of racist vitriol is not only endemic and the result of the fact you have a very wide but very shallow coalition, but second of all is almost inevitably transferred to the minorities about whom it is made. That's not good enough from side opposition in this debate. I'm going to bring you an argument as to how right-wing populism constitutes a fundamental affront to democracy and the rights of everyone else in a democracy who does not vote for these people, but before that five pieces of rebuttal. The first thing they say is that minorities don't actually feel afraid and the only evidence they give for this in their first speeches, oh you have some African-American women retweeting anti-Trump tweets. Two responses. First, they do not engage with the three lines we bring you in our first speech as to how it is actually horrific to have someone who is in their political incentives to stand up and say that you are a less valuable human being than someone else. But I challenge side opposition to tell the women who are raped by Duterte's army because he legitimizes that or for example the women who face rich-based abuse in the United States as a direct response of Trump's riots, the Mexicans who are beaten up after Trump rallies that they shouldn't be afraid in any meaningful sense of the word. Second thing they bring us is that these are voicing concerns that would otherwise be avoided. Four responses here. First, they concede in their very generic characterization of freedom of speech that and when they stand up and say we support things like hate speech that there are circumstances under which we are happy to tell people that they cannot vote for a particular individual. That is if we can prove to you that the harms to the wider society, i.e. the democratic norms of that society and specific minority groups are undermined. That is why they themselves do not believe that people should be able to vote for the neo-Nazi party and in doing so they concede that democracy has to operate within a range. Second of all, they give us no analysis to why specifically addressing the concerns of these people is unique to right-wing populists and rather just right-wing movements. We are pointing to, for example, the Republican Party in the US, the Conservatives in the United Kingdom, groups that attack the very kinds of policies that they stand for on their side of the house, things like having tighter immigration controls, but do so without saying that Mexicans are rapists. Third, this falls down because they fundamentally have to prove to you that the benefits accrued to these people are actually achieved. We gave you structural analysis in our first speech that has not been responded to as to how the shallowness of your coalition necessarily means you do not help the people in the Ross Belt. You do not help the people who fundamentally give you their democratic mandate. Third point of a rebuttal. They say these are legitimate policies. Again, two response. First, we would challenge the factual legitimacy of lots of the policies that individuals like Duterte or Trump have passed because the vast majority of them are not restricting trade or placing limits on the power of big businesses or giving more welfare to the working man. They are instead avoided because they recognise there is inherent conflict between supporting Obamacare for those that want it and also not supporting Obamacare for those in your mandate who do not want it. The kinds of policies you actually get passed are the ban on trans individuals in the army, the ban on Muslims from particular countries because those are the ones that is easiest to unite your base behind. Second of all, again, this is predicated on these policies actually being fulfilled and people actually wanting that. We point to the example of the fact that only 28% of United States citizens now supports Donald Trump as evidence of the fact that no matter what he says about these policies, he's probably not implementing them. Fourth and finally of all, they throw out this really generic argument about how this policy could possibly be abused because the vague criteria we implement. We tell you that the courts make exactly this kind of calculation the whole time and you know how we know that's effective because they stand by exactly the same mechanism when they talk about things like slander, when they talk about things like hate speech, when they talk about things like incitement to violence. If you can disseminate specific criteria and attach them to those instances of racial abuse, presumably we can also attach them to the five criteria we brought you in our first speech. Before I move on to my argument about how right-wing populism is fundamentally a front to everyone's rights. Yes. Come on, this obviously operates on a scale, right? So on the smaller end of things, if an individual person is racist and inciting other people to go and physically harm someone else, then possibly. If that person does so with 100,000 Twitter followers and the direct response to that policy is that hate crime increases 41% in the week after they are elected, yes. Okay, my argument, right-wing populism and the affronts democracy, we think right-wing populism undermines the integrity of democracy and is therefore a threat to the democratic rights of all. Two strands. First, it poisons real democratic debate. There are a couple of pieces of context we need to understand for this argument to make sense. First, right-wing populists abuse the truth and shift their stances. That is because they have broad but shallow coalitions brought together by conflicting vague promises, i.e. they promise simultaneous to slash Obamacare but also to provide welfare to those in need. We point to the example of Duterte as someone who literally has a team of 20 people hired to act as internet trolls and spread misinformation on the internet. Second of all, they dress up their opponents as conspiratorial elites and fundamentally use that as a tool to not engage with them. They demonise their portrayal of their opponents and as a consequence, when you try and challenge someone like Duterte or Trump or Gert Wilders on their prejudices, the response you get is, see, see, this is exactly what the liberal media is doing time and time again. Third thing on this, they rely on propagating fear and prejudice. Again, because they cannot unite their coalition through legitimate policy because the people they draw together are brought drawn under vague and conflicting terms, they are reliant on common fears and prejudices. The takeaway from this argument is that the key tenets, the key appeal of lots of these individuals is inherently irrational. That is to say they rely on the propagation of a fear that probably should not exist. All of these fundamentally mean undermine the debate necessary to productive, competitive electoral politics. Why? First, because when you twist and muddy the truth of a debate, you challenge the reality in response to which that debate existed in the first place. When fake news and alternative facts are placed on the same pedestal as actual factual realities, you cannot have a functional discussion about certain issues. You cannot have a functional discussion about undocumented workers in the United States when it is a fact that Mexicans are rapists in the same way that it is a fact that Nieto is a president of Mexico. Second of all, likewise, you also cannot have a debate between two reasonable sides that engage with each other and provide for the voter fundamentally a response. That is which of these two options is probably better for your interests. When one of those sides systematically fails to engage with the other, both of the consequence of the fact that they use literally who those other people are as a way of attacking them rather than attacking their policies. But second of all, they have terrorized the people that support them and struck fear into their hearts to extent that they will not hear logical reason. Second starting this argument is that these individuals attack the very framework of democracy. Part of their anti-establishment narrative on which they rely is to attack institutions like courts, like electoral processes. That is why we see individuals like Gert Wilders in the Netherlands and Donald Trump not only threatening to punish the courts if they get into power, but refusing to accept election results. Insofar as democracy relies on the balance of power that is supported both by the fact that you give a massive mandate to the individual that you give your vote to, but also by the fact that there are some checks and balances by an interdependent body to stop that person abusing that power. Democracy is fundamentally perverted when justices and the courts are fundamentally too afraid to do their job. The takeaway of this whole argument is twofold. First, if you weigh this debate based on democratic integrity and the rights of individuals, the very presence of right-wing populists makes it harder for people on the ground to come to terms with who they are voting for, why they are supporting that person and ultimately what they should draw out of that. We would draw the analogy to perjury and so far as we're happy to limit free speech in the short term if it ensures the integrity of a process that is vital to our society in the long run. So proud to propose.
judge_note: Compelling on equity and restrictions; vivid, slightly speculative. Clear structure, minor tangents. Delivery polished but moderate energy.

So proud.

Even when humans fuck up, even when humans cause problems, God has not intervened because he knows that the agency of humans is primary. Secondly, God created the initial universe in a way that was plentiful and balanced, where animals were able to feast on the nature that was in front of them and where people were able to thrive. I would note that the problems that are spoken about by prime minister are problems that are manufactured by humans. Things like racism and genocide were all created by humanity. This was never part of God's plan. But thirdly, God has a strong incentive to continue the existence of humanity even if he doesn't like this. Firstly, it probably gets a bit boring up in heaven. I think he needs something to entertain himself and something to watch. Secondly, if he didn't value humanity, he wouldn't create it. Why expend the effort? Why go and even bother? What does this mean? This means a few things in the context of the debate. Firstly, when God intervenes, he intervenes well. He intervenes benevolently, and he is likely to intervene to save humanity. I would note there are a variety of threats faced by humanity that humanity is not in a position to solve. Things like asteroids, over which we have literally no control because there are flying pieces of space debris that we have no control over and cannot stop. Things like climate change, which have gone so far off the rails that we cannot get them back on the rails again, where the earth is proposed to reach a tipping point very, very soon, and we cannot stop this due to immense amounts of lobbying and limited political capital to solve these problems. This means that God is able to intervene and God is likely to intervene. Secondly, I would note that the continuation of the universe doesn't equate the continuation of humanity. The universe may go on, but humans may be eliminated. As a consequence, we need at least one individual there who has the capacity to save humanity. If God created humans and God is all-powerful, God can save us. But finally, even if this is a lim—even if God doesn't—look, God has had limited interventions of the course of human history. Even if we save God for one intervention, even if God only intervenes once, if this intervention saves humanity, I think this intervention is of prime importance. Secondly, why is God yet not forsaken us? A Prime Minister talks about terrible things, things like genocide, things like war, things like hunger and starvation. I would note that these are horrible, and I would not contest that, but these aren't an existential risk. Even when World War II brought a lot of destruction and death to the planet, people still existed. In pockets of the world, people were untouched by the war, and people still continued to live. This means that God is likely to only intervene in the event that there is an existential risk. Secondly, I would note that a lot of these crises are current, and these crises are likely to get worse. There is a strong chance that God sees an obligation to protect future generations of the humans he sow values if these crises reach a tipping point. I show you already why they are likely to reach a tipping point. I think it is possible God intervenes in the short term. But thirdly, I think God is hopeful that so far humans will find their own way. He is hopeful that we will find our own solutions to these problems that we have created. As a consequence, there is a possibility that he could intervene in the face of existential risk. I would note that this is a very strong point to weigh in the debate. Insofar as any other benefits to humanity are contingent on humanity existing. When humans gamble as we do, God is a fail safe we cannot throw away. I would rather have one fail safe in the universe that could intervene than have none at all and be left to our own mercy and our own devices. Finally, why is it immoral to kill God? Firstly, I would posit that watching harms happen doesn't necessarily mean you are an evil person. Many of us stand here every day and watch horrible things happen in the world and yet we do not intervene and yet we do not do anything even when we have the capacity to. I would note that no one here would be considered evil as a consequence of that because often intervention isn't possible or often intervention isn't something that we feel as though we ought to do because we don't feel as though we should restrict someone else's agency. Secondly, life is a net moral good and God created us, therefore he created a vast amount of net moral good when he created us. Note that the negative things spoken of by opening government only exist because God gave us life and the capacity to develop these problems. Note that life is more utility positive than anything for three reasons. Firstly, all utility stems from life. Everything that we receive is contingent on us being alive. Standing in the stage, being in this auditorium is contingent on us being alive and waking up every day. Secondly, people have developed mental defense mechanisms of the course of human history like positivity bias to prevent us from getting demoralized and getting depressed and not waking up every day. Over time people evolved to view things positively and to defend themselves against negativity that exists. Last chance. Finally, everyone finds joy in nature and the lives of others. Sure. He has not intervened despite all the prayers given to him. How much more do you think we need to pray for him to intervene? God values agency. Even though people still pray, I think that God is only going to intervene at the point where people decide that they are going to kill themselves and there is an existential risk. I think insofar as we don't eliminate that existential risk, we are still in a pretty good spot. I would note this isn't the best choice to have to be making, but when humanity faces the existential risks that we face, this is a better choice than the other choice that we would do. Look, I think that insofar as God has created a world in which he gave us the faculties for good in the first place, to do all of the good things everyone wakes up and does every day, to hug children, to give birth, to love and to live as we do, I think to kill God would be immoral. Finally, many people believe in God. Even people who are spiritual still want to believe that something is there. God has brought hope to people when they can rationalize problems by saying God has a plan for us. Everything happens for a reason and you know you have the promise of salvation. Even if you were coerced into religion, you still got utility and meaning. Secondly, the afterlife still might not be there. There is a risk we destroy heaven for those who believe in the correct God and all we are left with is hell. In this way they will always be cutting the corners on this contract and fulfilling the bare minimum just so they can fulfill this contract and therefore reduce the costs as much as possible and therefore create as much profit as possible. In this way corporations will always have the incentive to fulfill the bare minimum. Moreover, no thank you, a lot of what actually takes place in these prisons and leads to successful rehabilitation is the intangible things as our opening speaker says, things such as rehabilitation that leads to the success of these prison services and these intangible things can't necessarily be written down in a contract and they are so important and therefore we tell you that this contract will not necessarily provide the services that lead to a successful prison. The second issue is that the corporate business structure will provide better services as they can provide a greater efficiency. The example here that they gave us was of ambulances and how when ambulance became privatised they were able to perform at a better standard. However, we tell you that ambulances, no thank you, are extremely different to prisons in that the goal of an ambulance is to pick up the patient and bring them to a hospital as fast as possible. Prisons are far, far more complex, there's hugely different range of purposes but make it far, far more difficult for it to be run under a corporate structure because when you're cutting the cost it actually leads to significantly different services and it can't just be measured by the time from getting to a hospital and therefore we tell you that these two issues aren't analogous. Why does the government not have the same motive to cut corners and save money? We tell you that the government doesn't have the same incentive to cut corners because they are not guided by this contract that tells them each of these points that they have to fulfil. They're actually looking at it from a holistic purpose in actually creating the best rehabilitation for these prisoners and they're not driven by getting the most money, no thank you. The third idea that we've heard from them is that the competition between corporations will be beneficial. I have two responses to this. Firstly, as our opening speaker said, this competition will always be driven by cost reduction and not necessarily by the services they provide. Secondly, we see that this competition is actually a really, really harmful thing. Imagine being halfway through a rehabilitation program and then a new institution comes in and takes over the prison and you have to switch to a new rehabilitation program. How is that at all going to be beneficial to you leaving the prison as a better person when your rehabilitation program has to be switched? We tell you, no thank you, that one of the most important things in a prison is having stability so that you can actually leave as a better person and stability is hugely important for rehabilitation and this competition will be extremely detrimental on this. No thank you. The final idea that we've heard from them as to why they bring better services for prisoners is that you can have more specialised prisons for particular demographics and that by bringing prisons close to the home you can have better rehabilitation as people can be in prisons with their friends. However, we tell you that being in a prison with your friends is only actually beneficial when the rehabilitation is successful and we tell you that it's far less likely that the rehabilitation will be successful when you're in a prison with your friends. Imagine going into a prison and your whole drug gang is there and they're all in prison with you and when you're ever going to be rehabilitated, when all the people that brought you into prison and called you to do these horrible things are right around you. In this way, even if the rehabilitation is successful, it's far less likely that it actually will be because you have all these things that brought you down first place. Yes please. The private space in a prison in America is a large government to have the revenue toys and dedicated drug rehab facilities and it's more of a better rehabilitation quality than it is and it's more of a better result for society to hold. Why do you ignore the government revenue that actually comes from this? We tell you that the main core of this argument is which is actually that creating better outcomes for prisons and we tell you that prisons are like the central idea and even if by putting the money elsewhere you'd be able to get better judicial systems and other things, that if that comes at the cost of having a successful prison system and it causes the successive prison systems to be reduced and that's something that we definitely don't want. Now moving on to my substantive material. Today I want to talk about the privatisation of prisons which would reduce the accountability of these prisons. So we tell you that under the status code we see that the government is able to be held accountable when failings take place. For example, if a prison to where to be released early due to good behaviour and they later re-appended, then the government would be held accountable. So why does this change when prison becomes privatised? Two reasons. Firstly, corporations have a greater incentive to sweep issues under the rug because when the prison is relying on their reputation to be able to uphold their profit and their business structure, they will want to sweep these issues under the rug as soon as they possibly can. The second reason is that privatised prisons also have a greater means to push these issues under the rug. They can afford and have the ability to have PR agents that can give them quality media spin when these failings come out and therefore help them get rid of these issues quickly. In this way we see a diminished level of accountability in privatised prisons than under the status code. Thank you.
judge_note: Strong accountability and stability case; persuasive, slightly speculative. Clear structure. Delivery polished with minimal pauses.
