That means that you are more likely to have incentives to treat prisoners badly. Thirdly, vigilante justice is often rampant in these communities, therefore giving civilians more power and empowering them to dictate how criminal justice system works is always a bad idea, so we win this on principle. But secondly, the practical outcomes you would get because of that low bar to start with are so small that they are almost negligible. Yes, please. Why would people who have been failed by the government their entire lives trust a government? Get a get on to that. Thirdly, quality. Alright, this is kind of where the debate really fell down because you didn't really hear from either side what anybody wanted to achieve in this debate, into the latter half of this debate where we had both sides telling you we wanted to get better quality standards of prisons. So firstly, let's talk about incentives and why they matter. Already talked about why prisons and crime is a huge cost to the government. What we're hearing from the opposition is open ambulances. Here's the difference, you pay for private healthcare, that is the difference, so they obviously have an incentive to provide a very good service for you because you are paying for that service. Prisoners do not pay you to go to prison, that is the fundamental difference in that case. That made no sense. Secondly, let's talk about our extension, which is this idea of variance between prisons. So what we're hearing from the opposition is, well, you get specialized prisons. What we tell you is, no, you get some really good prisons where you do pop, like, maybe in the best case scenario of their world, some people who care about prisoners, note they will be white collar prisons in which areas because that's where you have the most political capital. And then you get the prisons that are entirely run off of our incentives. And I'm both hearing from the opposition, by the way, let's just take a little bit logical here. The opposition told us that, like, oh, you have a contract where you have to get the same services for less money or better services for less money. Okay, let's talk about something like food. Let's say I have to feed 5,000 people and I have to feed them like a decent meal. And I say, hey, I can do it for like $10,000 and hey, I can do it for $7,000. Obviously, if you're only doing it for $7,000, then it's going to be a worse service. I did not understand any characterization of the world in which you could get a better service for a cheaper price. That make no sense. That doesn't happen in the real world. That was always going to be bullshit. Sorry, that was always going to be untrue in this debate. And finally, let's talk about rehab because that was also particularly important in this debate. And this is where this idea of trust comes in, right? Because if you don't trust the system that you're in, you're not going to engage with the problem, you're not going to be able to be rehabilitated properly. I don't really think that a petty drug deal on the street that's been sent to prison three times cares who's running the prison. I think that person's probably just pretty annoyed to be in prison. I think that's probably why they're upset. I don't think it's because the government is running that prison for them. But I also think that if they're in a prison that has been overcrowded, where they're getting less food, where they don't have as much, like, services are not being required as well and where prison standards generally go down, which was actually conceited by the end of the debate, but they're probably going to be even less happy with the government at that point for sending them to prison in the first place. That's why you're going to get less trust. That's why you're going to get less engagement. I've spoken for way too long. I'm very proud.
judge_note: Compelling responsibility and variance summary; persuasive, slightly speculative. Effective structure. Delivery fast but pause- and filler-heavy.

motion: This house would allow citizens to opt public services for tax brakes.
speech_text: Mr Speaker, we think it is absolutely despicable that our government is stealing people's money and unfairly distributing them to those who can afford it, sorry, those who can afford otherwise but choose not to. We think it is incredibly unfortunate that we can't stand behind a solution or a system that disadvantages low ACS people, that disadvantages rural citizens whilst benefiting rich people who can afford private schools, private health insurance and other services that they should be paying for themselves. We support a system in which all Australians and all citizens can benefit from the services that government provides or be able to provide for their own services and we see that the waste that exists in the current system and the low quality of services provided in the current system can be solved really easily by adopting our model. And this is why we think that people should be able to opt out of public services in return for tax breaks. So before I go on to my point, I want to explain what this model looks like in the world we want to tell you about. So we think that people should be able to opt out of services such as health, services such as education and other basic limited infrastructure services. So we think that this looks like people paying reduced taxes for certain services, people not having to pay their Medicare levy if they want to opt out of Medicare, people having to pay for toll roads if they want to use services like that. And we think that the best way to measure these form of tax breaks, no thank you, the best way to measure these form of tax breaks is make them proportional to the amount that the government would have otherwise spent on these services, we think that tax money. On that point, no thank you. If anything, we think that in certain circumstances we would rather this be underestimated than overestimated because we don't think that would be like a massive incentive for people to change whether or not they opt out of the system. No thank you. So we want to clarify that the system doesn't look like people not paying taxes because they want to pull out of services, we think they're still like a certain amount of services that aren't really hard to opt out of. But broadly we think that it looks like tax breaks, it looks like a reduction in taxes, not paying for things like Medicare levy. No thank you. So I'm going to bring you two points of some status. Why people have a right to opt out of these kind of services under our model. And secondly, why this improves public services and improves the quality of public service provided. At Deputy Prime Minister, we will tell you why this leased-to-beverage service is provided in the private sector and why this improves the quality of services received by rural people and rural citizens at that point. No thank you. That's my first point about why individuals have a right to opt out of this current system and adopt our model. We think on the most basic level, people know best about their own needs, their own health, their own education, their own infrastructure needs and their own services that they require to fulfill their life. No thank you. We think that the government doesn't know everything about the citizens, the need of the citizens, so we think on this basic level, individuals know more about what kind of services they should receive and what they should be paid for their services at the point. I'll take you Chris. Rich people pay more than they get back in tax moments so they have incentive to opt out. That means the poorest will get even lower quality services, doesn't it? We disagree with that proposal because we think that under our system, there will be more funds, there will be more resources to be distributed to these people who need that kind of money the most. We think that under our model, this money can be used more effectively to the smaller number of people who need these services more and it can be better used to ensure that these citizens receive a high quality of services and those who opt out of the system will receive a high quality of services as well. Secondly, we think that people who don't benefit from the system and these services shouldn't have to pay for them. We think that under the system where the government takes people's money and redistributes it to people in a way that doesn't really address the needs of individuals and what those individuals can afford is a really damaging system. We believe in a principle where the individual knows what they need, the individual knows their basic requirements of society and that they should be able to pay for them in whatever way they want to. We think there's a user pay system where people pay for the exact services they receive whereas as they can't afford those services, the government provides those services and intervenes to ensure that they do receive the quality of services that they deserve. No thank you. Now to my second point in this debate about why this will improve the quality of public services provided to citizens. So within one level, people who can afford these services will get off these services because they can afford a better form of private insurance or a better form of private education that the government service can't currently provide because it doesn't have the resources to provide those services because it's flooded with a number of people wanting these services. So we think that under our model, under our system, people will get off these services because they know they'll have a better quality access, a better service provided to them in terms of education, health and other forms. What this means is that there are less people demanding these services. There will be more money and there will be better specialisation for the government to provide these services for the remaining people who can't afford to get onto different services. What this means is that there is more money, more resources directed at fewer people demanding these services, which means that the per capita or per person amount of resources being able to be spent on these services improves. So this improves the quality of services for people who are most vulnerable. For those people who can't afford to go on alternative services because they don't have the wealth to afford things like private education and private health insurance, what this system does is ensure that these individuals still have a high quality access to high quality services provided by the government. Secondly, we think that it provides the government an incentive to run better services so people don't opt out of these services. So we think that under these circumstances, the government has an incentive to raise money, to provide better services, to maintain popular support to provide these things to citizens. Secondly, we think that if people believe that the services they get when they opt out, sorry, the services they don't get, the services they have currently are better than the services they get when they opt out, we think that in these circumstances people won't opt out because the services they receive are of a high enough quality. So we think that government providing this incentive to people by approving services because they want to have money, because they want to maintain their popular support, we think it's a large incentive for the government to ensure that as many people as possible stay on these services and that's just don't like they get as much money as possible, which is why the government has an incentive to provide the best possible services it can. So ultimately because we think that people have a right to opt out of the system, because we think this improves the quality of services provided to individuals and citizens, we believe that people should be able to opt out of public services in return for tax breaks.
judge_note: Clear model, persuasive on choice; slightly speculative. Structured but tangent-prone. Delivery energetic but filler-heavy.

motion: This house would allow citizens to opt public services for tax brakes.
speech_text: On our side of the house, we believe that people already opt out of public services and we think that they should. We don't think that opting out of public services means that they don't have to pay for them. This is because we think it's very, people get a lot of benefit from public services they don't necessarily use. We think your results are better educated, a healthier work force and we think that on our side of the house we're always happy to stand by people being generally better off under the public system. So several points over a bottle firstly. Why services are actually worse off under this model in terms of the public and private sector? Looking at the public sector, we think that under this model services will be targeted to the rich to get them to opt into that system, to get their tax dollars to make sure that they pay for that public system. We think what you end up with is a public system that then caters towards that middle class and upper class who might opt out anyway and that public system is then no longer able to cater for those that have to use the public system, those that aren't able to opt out and maybe have to suffer under a public service that doesn't necessarily cater for them and what they need. Secondly, we think that the Prime Minister said that public services were already considered bad and that's why people opt out. We think they're only going to be worse under your side of the house because maybe in the short term having less people in the public system is a good thing because maybe then there will be more hospital beds. We think what this means in the long term is that there will be no money, like no tax dollars going into that public health system which means that the infrastructure there will be no room for infrastructure to grow, there will be no money for that public service to then get better as the population grows in the long term as maybe more people need to go on the public service. There will be no money to improve those services and make them better in the long term for people that cannot opt out. We think that there's only a situation on your side of the house, no thank you, we think that's really bad, we're not prepared to stand for that. Secondly, the Prime Minister wanted to talk about where the government will get their money from to make these services better. In the real world, people pay for these public services such as Medicare, the public health system and then opt out and go into the private health care system and we think that's fine because that excess money that they've paid for, no thank you, to go to Medicare is the money that is then able to support those people who can't necessarily pay for the public health care system but can't opt out into anything else. We think when that is where the majority of funding towards those public health care systems are going is from that excess, that wastage that the Prime Minister wanted to call it. You're not going to get money from anywhere else, we think that all you end up doing is making the no thank you, the public health care system works off because there's less money. Okay, now onto my substantive points. Firstly, why you have an obligation to pay for things you don't use directly and why this will actually benefit you in the long term. Secondly, why some people can't necessarily opt out of some services and thirdly, the harms to public services in the future. Firstly, why people have an obligation to pay for things they don't directly use? We think that people as citizens firstly have an obligation, no thank you, in paying taxes to support people that can't necessarily support themselves due to structural barriers that potentially limit their social mobility. We think because people benefit from these public services, even if they don't use them, such as the public health care system, where people under that system can then be better, healthier workers for a company, for example, or in the public education system, no thank you, where you'll get better, more productive, more educated workers in the future, you probably mitigate the chances of those children going into poverty. No thank you, that's better off for those children and those people in the public health care system. We think that it is an obligation, and I'll take you in a moment, we think it is an obligation for citizens of a state to provide for those people who can't provide for themselves yet. So do you think that people opting out of the current system and going to think that private health care, private education is a form of them, like using those social mobility to receive better services for themselves? I don't think so because I think in the long term it's really harmful for people to opt out of these public health care systems because there will be no ability for that system to ever get better in the long term, there will be no funds for that. And we think that that is actually going to be more detrimental to those people who have limited social mobility because now not only do they have limited social mobility in and of itself, that the public health care or public education system that they can't opt out of is actually worse for them. My second point, why some people can't necessarily opt out of some services, no thank you. We think it's quite a short term calculus to make, and that often people can't make where they have to balance out the short term benefit of maybe saving some, or getting some sort of tax break at the end of the next financial year versus the uncertainty of maybe not having a public service down the road that they might actually need, and we didn't hear anything from their side of the house about how people could re-opt in, no thank you, to public services that they decide in the future that maybe they really need. We think it's really harmful for people who potentially opt out of public health care, and then mid-heart attack realise that they need an ambulance but can't get one because they've opted out of the public health care system a couple of years before. We think it is very dangerous for their side of the house, no thank you, to make this short term calculus and dangle this carrot of the tax break in front of them when they don't really have an idea of what they'll need in the future. On to my last point, why this harms the public services, yes Charlotte? Did private services generally run more efficiently and better than public services? We think that even if they do, people still have an obligation to do whatever they can, whether that is tassels, whether it is opting into that system, to make the public health care system better. We don't think necessarily that all private systems work better than the public system, and that's because a lot of the time, a lot of money is directed into these public services to make them better. Also, generally more people in a system means that you can get more specialised, more varied services, we think that's a really good thing that you only get an outside of the house. So very quickly, my last point, like I said in a point of rebuttal to the Prime Minister, the problem with this model is that in the short term the infrastructure has a capacity and maybe even is better to support fewer people in this system. But in the long term, there is less money in the system and fewer people after maybe the rich or the middle class opt out. With population growing, the lack of money in the form of income tax or payments to this public health care system thus reduces the ability of public services to invest in future infrastructure. If there is a population growth, then there will be no money to buy hospital beds for more people in the future. We think this is most harmful to the people that are stuck in this public system that can't opt out, that can't just go to something that they consider better because of some structural barriers. We think that the biggest harms to the public services actually occur under their side of the house because it prevents them from getting better and it is the most detrimental to people who have no alternative. We think dangling this short term calculus in front of people, when we have an obligation to provide for future public services, as I outlined in my first point, it's very harmful and harms the most vulnerable and poor in the long term for those reasons, proud to.
judge_note: Strong funding and obligation case; persuasive, slightly speculative. Clear structure. Delivery fluent but filler-heavy.

motion: This house would allow citizens to opt public services for tax brakes.
speech_text: I have three main questions. One, whether this system increases or decreases social mobility and whether that's a good thing. Whether things will get better in the long term and whether there will be money in the system later. Thirdly, what happens when people opt out of the system and whether they can opt back in. Moving on to my first point, with social mobility, the opposition came out with this idea that we are excluding people from this sort of basis system and that because of that they will get worse public services and as a result they'll be stuck there at the bottom forever. However, we feel like this will actually improve their social mobility especially for people on the cusp as a specific stakeholder. If you're wanting to send your kid to private school but you just can't because it's just a little bit out of your budget, you can end up actually sending them with the benefit of this tax break. As well as with increased competition within the private sector because there are more people opting into going into the private sector, costs will be lower so allowing these people on the cusp to opt into better private schools and public services. Moving on to my second question, whether things will get better in the long term. To that we have two sort of responses. One, saying that privatization generally is a good thing and it's a lot better for everyone especially as I will address later why it glows costs and why it becomes more accessible to more people. We believe that this is generally an outcome that society does indeed want. The second thing being that there will still be money in the tax system. We are not saying that just because you're not saying, no thank you, that you're just sending your kid to private school now you don't have to pay any tax. There's still going to be better, there's still going to be money to divert to public services and because we have less people we can somewhat specialize to the people that are in the lower SES backgrounds and have this sort of need for better public services. No thank you. As my first speaker brought out and how I will further reiterate in my speech. Moving on to my third point, what will happen when people opt out? We believe that people are generally rational and that they're not going to opt out and spend a wonderful tax break on a holiday or something. They're definitely going to instead of opting out of public health services they will get private health insurance. That way as the opposition brought out if they are suddenly having a heart attack they do have private insurance with which to deal with it. However if there is a further concern it's quite easy to adopt into a model where you have to ensure that people have to take no thank you, have to have private insurance before they can opt out to show that they are willing to do this. Moving on to my two points. Given that you recognize that some people who would otherwise be giving money to these systems are opting out the overall funding for these public services will go down. How do you say that the quality of those services will stay the same or even increase? I think that we can say that for two reasons. One because we still are going to have tax dollars from other sort of tax incomes as well as the second thing being there's less people in the system and that's all over better for the system. As my first speaker brought up that the system can become a bit more specialized and it can work better in dealing with the problems of those people and in that way the infrastructure will get better or at least more specialized while privatization continues to improve the sort of standards that other people may not have gotten because public services are so widely used. Moving on to my first point, I'm going to have two points. One being that private services are a good thing and why having a private system in our model is overall better. My second point being that how it's going to affect regional stakeholders and how that is good. Firstly, privatization is generally a good thing. Most people will admit that. We believe that more people will be able to get into the system. People will be wanting to opt into a private system and as a result there's going to be more competition, more demand within that private system. So that's going to have two things. A, it's going to lower the cost and therefore yes. How many people on a below median income can afford private services? I can't exactly quote out statistics for you but I believe that there are definitely some stakeholders who are definitely on the cost and having a tax break would definitely allow them to get private services. As well as under our model, the more people that are wanting private services, the better the private services will get and the more cost affordable they will get. I will explain that just right now if you really want to. The idea is that there's going to be more competition because there's going to be more people opting in. There's going to be more profits in that sort of area and so private companies are going to be more inclined to get into that industry and sort of be more competitive. So in doing that they're going to have two things. They're going to lower the cost because as a result like privatization, there's more incentive in a private company to lower costs and be efficient because they are essentially profit-driven. As it stands, public services aren't profit-driven and there's no sort of incentive for them to lower costs and become better. The second thing is that there's going to be better privatization. Sorry, they're going to gain to the needs of more people because there's going to be sort of an incentive to capture markets and in doing that you're going to make sure that you include more sort of a wider market spread and in doing that you're going to make private services more accessible for everyone. Just quickly moving on to my regional point, because it's allowing certain people to opt out, this means that regional people have two options. Either A, they can opt out if their services are not being provided within their area, which is currently a huge issue as there aren't very many public services provided in regional backgrounds, but as well if more people in a sort of metro area opt out, the government then can realize that the people paying taxes are the people in the regional area and then provide better quality education and health services to the people in a regional area. And for that reason we are proud to support the motion.
judge_note: Compelling mobility and competition case; vivid, slightly speculative. Clear structure, minor tangents. Delivery steady but pause-heavy.

motion: This house would allow citizens to opt public services for tax brakes.
speech_text: Honourable Speaker, on our side of the house what we tell you is that under the current system people opt out anyway and that it's okay for people to opt out but what it isn't okay Madam, Mr. Speaker, what it isn't okay for these people to do is to opt out and then not pay for what they've opted out of because what this leads to will be a lower quality of public service overall and we think this is something incredibly damaging, incredibly harmful for society as a whole. I have four points in rebuttal and then two points in substantive which I'll try and get through very quickly. First of all we were told that people have a right to opt out by the Prime Minister but what my Leader of the Opposition told you is that people currently do opt out and we support that right to opt out but what under our side of the house we don't support is the fact that people then don't have to pay for opting out of these things. We tell you that people must pay because overall what people benefit from paying for things that they don't use. People will benefit just because we may send our children to a private school. We start to pay for public education because in the long term having a more educated workforce, having a more healthy workforce by paying your Medicare levy, what this leads to is a better outcome for society overall for people who might not necessarily use these services. We tell you that I'll take you in a moment. We tell you that these people will benefit overall in the long term. So we tell you that the right to opt out is okay, that people still must be paying. I'll take you now. Do you think that under the current system where people opt out of these services and still receive a lot of government funding like in private schools is better than our model where people opt out of these systems will have to pay for themselves? We tell you absolutely yes, this is better under our model because what this means is that people will still be receiving the funding. We'll still have a lot of funding there for public services that are going to be provided where under your model this funding reduces and even though some people might benefit slightly a little bit more in the private system, we actually will still have the money. We still will have the funding for the public system which under your model reduces which we think is incredibly harmful. Under my second point of rebuttal, we were told that better public services would be provided if we privatised these industries. We were told that there's an incentive for the government to run better services so people don't opt out. But what we tell you is that privatisation is not a good thing in this situation. We tell that it's incredibly limiting and that people don't always have a right to opt out of basic tools and services. No thank you. Because what happens when we start privatising things honourable speaker is that this isn't good for the public, it is good for the market, it is self-serving and when we are seeing large scale sorts of services like healthcare, like education, what we tell you is that these are things that the government needs to run. These are things that are run more efficiently, these are things that are run better when the government is involved than when the private system is involved. Because what happens is that the private system located to the market, I'll take you in a moment, and not the people. We tell you this is incredibly harmful because when private systems run at a loss, people aren't taken into account but when the public system runs at a loss, it's something that's okay and something that's sustainable. Governments are often really bad at updating to modern times, each year Australia Post. Don't you think it would be a good thing if we could all just shut down Australia Post, like all just refusing to buy into that service and force the government to modernise? We tell you that no, we don't think that's a good thing. Even though Australia Post might often not run as efficiently as it can, we tell you this is a service that works and is efficient, works for the people. In these situations when we're talking about education, we're talking about health care, it's incredibly important for the government to do this. The private market will make it more harmful overall. And this is something that we can't stand for on our side of the house. We then tell you that opting out will lead to people spending money. We were told by the member for the government that when people opt out, what this will mean is that the tax breaks that they would have otherwise be paying for will lead to people spending money on things like better health care and private health care. But what we tell you is that the money won't actually be directed there. So the money involved in that system overall will absolutely decrease because what these people will be treating this money as is like a tax break. They will be spending it on holidays overseas. They will be spending it elsewhere. And the money available for the public system and the private system, no thank you, will decrease overall and we think this is incredibly, incredibly harmful. So onto my point of why this will make the system worse overall. What we tell you first is that when we reduce the amount of people involved in the public system, people when we allow people to opt out of the system and it becomes smaller, we tell you this is incredibly harmful because what it means is that people won't be able to demand specialisation in that service, in that industry. We tell you that people won't be able to have the amount of resources, have those things sort of tailored to them. No thank you. When we see people, the amount of people opting out of that system because people will just, what will happen is that the kind of lowest common denominator, if you will, will just be catered for. We won't see this kind of specialisation and we won't see these kind of opportunities. No thank you for everyone in that system. We also tell you that it will make the system worse because what happens is that we encourage the system to be targeted to the rich. We tell you that the government will be aiming these, trying to get the rich to stay in the system, to kind of pay for the private healthcare, public healthcare system. And this is incredibly, no thank you. Harmful when we don't allow people, if we let this to happen under their side of the house. We then tell you that it's incredibly harmful for people in, no thank you, in a lower tax bracket because whilst it might look like a carrot in the short run, by letting people have a tax bracket, they'll be saving money by not paying for healthcare. In the long run, when people will need these services because when people eventually will need healthcare, people eventually will need education, they won't have a chance to opt back in. We tell you it's very harmful to hold a carrot out in the short run for these people when it has very, very long term harmful results. So for these reasons and for the reasons that my first week is presented to you with, we are proud to oppose.
judge_note: Strong funding and specialization case; persuasive, slightly speculative. Tight structure. Delivery fluent but filler-heavy.

motion: This house would allow citizens to opt public services for tax brakes.
speech_text: The thing about government services is a lot of the time people genuinely morally object to them and their provision. They genuinely disagree with what the state is doing and under the status quo the state uses its coercive power of the law to force people to pay for things that they don't just, what do we disagree with and particularly enjoy, but actively morally oppose. That makes those things morally complicit to a financial level and the activities of the state. And the most obvious example is abortion services divided out of public health care hospitals, right? Because the line is that abortion is a private matter, is a bit of a lie when you're forcing everyone around you to pay for that same health care system that provides those abortions. We think on either side of the house people should in democratic systems have the right to tell, to say their government is doing the wrong thing and I will opt out of the service and your government service provision and get a discount back or not be financially complicit in that activity based on that fact and that will form the large portion of our extension in this debate. So what am I going to do? I'll start off with some of that and there's going to be two parts to my extension. I'll start by extending out by explaining about the morality of service provision and why the line to be opt out is really important and then secondly I'll talk about and do some extending in and talk about why we think this provides better service provision. But first some lying things of rebuttal. One, the opening up told us that people derive general benefit from things like an educated society and an ever society with health care. And that's if we will still generally pay tax on the right side of the house. People will not support their education system to some extent and they will roughly pay and I'll take it in a second, for the general benefits they've arrived, what they will get back is the specific benefit they will have otherwise arrived from the system. That's why they will still pay self tax, they will not pay as much tax they otherwise would have. Why do we require them to remain and contribute to the society? Why can't we just leave for someone that we are morally opposed to? I mean like, so that's a bit of a like a there's a circular problem there, right? And if you assume the state has the power to demand that we only even the reason the state controls me in the first place. Obviously in this debate, our extension is not people should be able to opt out of the state entirely, right? But there are specific instances where it's a nice thing to do, just like people not paying the services they disagree with. There's a bit more value in which they can use their wallet as an expressionary tool. They don't think people should be able to opt out of the entire system to the state, nor is that a viable thing that people have the capacity to do. Nor can a state let that happen. motion: This House as Rational People of the World, regret the hanging of Adolf Eichmann.
speech_text: There were two major points coming from the Prime Minister's speech. How this is an unfair trial and how the discourse has been generated is bad. I'm going to tackle both these points at the start of my speech itself. This wasn't an unfair trial at all. When you try someone in a legal system, where you're trying them not for their intentions, but for the actions and the atrocities committed against you, right? The reason we convict people in the places where they have been committing those crimes is because that's the only way to ensure fair justice. When Aajma Kusup was tried in Indian courts, it was better because we actually got results against Aajma Kusup or we actually got to hang Aajma Kusup because of the atrocities he committed against India in 2016-11. But we couldn't have Naab Hafiz Said, who is a mastermind behind the 2016-11 attacks, who is still sitting in his home in Pakistan and not being accountable for his crimes. His crimes have been absolved and such. We don't think having a fair trial somewhere else will be something as fair. We think that the people who the atrocities are committed against are the people who should be conducting this trial and we think this is a completely fair trial given the context and everything else as well. The discourse being generated is bad. Look at Germany post World War I and look at Germany post World War II. The discourse happening in Germany post World War I was something of resentment. It was something that they hate everyone else. It was something that it's us against the world and everything else. That's the kind of discourse that was being generated. And the discourse generated after World War II because of the atrocities committed against Germany because of the retributive actions created against the people who were in the Nazis in Germany after World War II, this massive outrage and retribution was something that was essentially needed to push them in the right direction. We think that Germany now has taken legal action against all these people. Look at Germany now today. You cannot go around and flaunt your naagism there. Naagism isn't tolerated over there in terms of media discourse itself or any discourse itself. You will not be tolerated there if you are a Nazi. This sort of discourse has been the kind of fear that was generated for being associated with being a Nazi, for being associated with being associated with atrocities is what essentially led to the world becoming more tolerant. It's what led to Germany becoming more accepting and more tolerant of this. Because we pushed them to an extent and said that these crimes are bad. The crimes you committed against these Jews are so bad, that's why they have to understand why this is happening. That's why we show these atrocities are committed on a basis of ethnic cleansing or racism, something that shouldn't be tolerated and something that should require strict and firm action. This is what the legal system does. It's supposed to act as a deterrence of these sort of crimes. You will only have a deterrence when you actually have a proper trial and a proper way of handling justice to these people. Let's get to my concerns. During war time, we think that even in times of peace, when you commit war crimes, people still have to be accountable for their war crimes. Even if you had the action of pulling the trigger and you were just following orders, you would throw me X-Men, I would tell you what happened when Star Wars was first awakened. There was a guy called Finn. He was supposed to follow orders and go shoot these people because of what happened in the first order. He didn't go do that. Even though he was coerced by the first order, even though he was trying to push that, he still had an active choice. He still had an active choice not to pull the trigger. He's the one who didn't shoot any of those people there and he's the one who took the right decision. While they may say that's coercion, and when coercion is still there, we still think that there's an active choice and we still judge a person by their actions and not by their intentions. At the end of the day, ultimately what will happen is that it's still a person dragging all the students and beating them up. It's still a person pushing them and shoving them into concentration camps. Even if that person is mindlessly brainwashed, he still has some sort of... He still has sentient brain. He still knows what the fuck he's doing. He's not someone who's completely brainwashed into being a robot who's not pushing anyone else. There is still a moral value here. There's still some sort of moral inside his brain. This sort of brainwashing happens to a coercion to an extent, but it's not something that removes his own moral compass within him. His own moral compass within him thinks that those things are not bad, then he should be committed to having for those things anyways. That's the whole point of this debate. What we also want to tell you is that it's fair for the judicial system to give him an asset trial. He was skilled on the grounds that the atrocities committed and not for the intention of which he committed these atrocities. You have to understand that the people who gave orders are also being tried. There's a collective blame here. It's not just a single person being blamed here. If that was the case, by their definition, you just kill Hitler for killing the 6 million Jews and there's no other action to be committed here. But there is a collective blame and a systematic order of hierarchy which exists today and which exists in those war crimes. And every one of those has to be nabbed. It has nabbed all those people and given them the appropriate punishments for the crimes committed. Given the fact that he was a food soldier, the appropriate punishment for the hierarchy was justified because of how many people he would have killed or because of how many people he would have shoved into his concentration camps. So we think it's completely justified for them to do so. It's not the fact that you have to just take the guy on top who's giving the orders but it's the people who actually committed the atrocities too because they are actively involved in committing those atrocities before I move on. Sir, taking your Star Wars example, in 8 movies of Star Wars, apart from the one undocumented dissent of Finn, how many dissent of Finn? It's not about one documented dissent of Finn. It's about having an active choice. You still have an active choice. The example of Finn was broad because of the fact that the choice is still there. If Finn didn't dissent, there wouldn't be any active choice. But that is an example to show you that they still have an active choice. Just because the other people haven't exerted an active choice, it doesn't mean they're absorbed all the crimes they've committed against humanity, against the people, against the Jews and everything. We think they're still accountable for the crimes they've committed. We think that something should help. Getting back to what I was talking about, how the hierarchy is being cut down anyway, it's not about the justice being given to those people who the crimes have been committed against. But that's not the case. We're talking specifically about the crimes committed against them in the scenario of war. This person has specifically committed these acts against these Jews. We're not talking about the oppression faced by Jews. We're talking about the specific acts of crimes that have been committed. In this regard, if this person who has been accused of committing these specific crimes against the Jews is tried and is successfully convicted of committing these crimes, he cannot give us the excuse that he was following orders because acts of bullsh**t ex-jews. We don't think that he didn't have an active choice. We think that even though he was a footman who was pulling a trigger, he still had a role in the crime. He's still someone who was doing the crime as well. And also the fact that them talking about how Israel is something of a retributive society at that point of time. motion: This House as IRA will save FARDEEN. - INFO: Space Cadet Petrushka (pronounced in a Russian accent) has been tasked with monitoring the Sirius Star Sector. While visiting an unremarkable system of a yellow dwarf star, she spots a planet inhabitated by a sentient species with space-faring capability. This planet's dominant species is hyper-aggressive and has brought the planet itself to the brink of ruin. There is massive ruin of the environment, natural surroundings and war and aggression in this species is rampant. Petrushka has a certain technology which she can choose to give to new planet and its inhabitants which will save them from ruin. Without this technology, the planet and its inhabitants (FARDEEN) are doomed.
speech_text: Madam Speaker, to quote one of my favorite Hogwarts professors in this situation, help shall always be given to those who deserve it. And not in this situation to those who need it. And that remains the burden of us in this debate. Before we move forward, what we have to tell you in this debate as side opposition is essentially two things. One, that this species, 13, does not deserve our help. And second, that even if you do help them, the harms that arrive out of this move are much greater than any sort of marginal benefit that you might be talking about. And hence this move on behalf of the Federation is stupid. Right? Having established what my burden in this debate is, let's talk about the first constructor that came out in this situation from closing government. They spoke about this fantastic system of imposing values upon something. Let's understand this. The minute you say that you are going to impose your values like you did upon the natives, you make a fundamental concession in your case, which is that these natives are unfit to join the Federation in their current state. Iraq, by extension of Petrushka's essential function is to save those planets which in their current state are fit to join the Federation. So we tell you as side opposition that saying that has already lost you the entire debate. What we tell you in this situation is when you tell us that we agree they are unfit in their current state and that we will impose our value systems upon them, you are making concession to the idea that yes, they are unfit and therefore the function of Iraq which is to decide whether the current situation they are fit or unfit to join the Federation is decided. You essentially accept that yeah, they are unfit and therefore we win this debate on that point. But moving forward on our second system of how we are going to extract benefits. I think that side of government here has misconstrued what the idea of a Federation is. We are not talking about a Klingon Empire. We are talking about the Federation in start like. The Federation is not this fantastic British Empire which colonizes people and extracts their resources and stuff like that. No, no, the Federation is a bargaining table. The Federation is a body which comes, the Federation is more like the United Nations, not like the German Empire and World War II. We tell you that the Federation in this situation is something at which people will come to the same table. So once you do save them, once you make them aware of the Federation which essentially gives them access to the Federation because of the space space and capabilities, you are not going to colonize them. Instead you are going to give those aggressive people a seat on your table and you are here to tell you that that's going to fuck you up. That's going to fuck us up essentially. Having established that, let's move on to certain characterizations which have been done very well. We need to establish that. What we need to establish in this situation is that the Federation, we have looked at this debate from the perspective of people and my language, people are far from being a bad. What we have completely missed out in this situation is the perspective of the Federation which is us, which is what we achieve in this situation. So before we move on to the consequential analysis, the real bit of moral aspect that we have to bring about in this situation is a little bit of Neutral morality. Where we tell you that this is not about morality of saving people and all of that, which you have spoken about, that we should save people because we can save people. We tell you that no. The morality is essentially similar to what Neutral proposed in his book Beyond Good and Evil when he spoke about slave morality which is in fact the most dominant morality in the world today. Which is to establish that your morality of actions is decided by the consequences that arise out of our actions and not by the morality of the actions in themselves. So we tell you that the morality of this action is essentially to be judged upon what benefit I achieve by this action in this situation, meaning the Federation in this situation. And hence we are telling that this debate is strictly limited only to consequential analysis of the benefit that I achieve by this situation. And that's what the moral aspect in this argument is. But moving on to the consequential analysis that we have to provide in this situation. We have already on both sides made this concession that the people of Urbine have found out that the characterization of people of Urbine is such that they are hyper aggressive people who are not capable of sitting at the same table with their own species and trying to work together. Trying to save people, trying to save planet earth in this situation. First tackling opening government which had this fantastic idea that you will give them a pill. The question which I asked in this situation was not like whether you are going to give them a pill because one that's how the debate is supposed to be about. But even if you do give them a pill, the logical link that you try to draw is once you curb their aggressiveness, they are suddenly going to become super smart, super cooperative and stop climate change and stop all the other destruction that they have caused to their planet already and suddenly like it's going to be a new rejuvenated planet which is going to work. Which we tell you is a link that you did not offer us therefore we don't stand by that. And second when you spoke about the benefits that we are going to achieve in this movie tell you that one those are benefits an empire achieves not a federation so that's not within the mandate of a federation. But let me tell you what harms are going to come out from the fair part of the federation in this situation right. So now essentially we tell you that the minute you save them, the minute you make them aware of the actions of the federation given that they have spacefaring capabilities this means that you are essentially inviting them to join us on the table you are inviting them to the federation. So this debate is whether we should invite them to the federation or not having already dealt with the fact that Ferdinand should not be saved. Now whether if they are saved what are the benefits or what are the harms that come out of inviting them to our table right. And the harms that come out are primarily already about the aggressiveness of the species of Ferdinand given that they have not been able to sit with their own species at a table and like discuss right. Given that like black and white people in that situation have not been able to solve it out. How do you think they are going to react when they see random aliens like fish heads and like other tentacle things and all that right. We tell you that no given that they are already hyper aggressive in nature these are not the kind of people who can sit with you on the table and discuss peace discuss a court discuss trade treaties and all of that because these are essentially like characterized earlier greedy people who are going to grab whatever they get who are going to want as much who are going to like take as much as they can get out of the situation. And the biggest harm that comes because of their space varying capabilities is now they are going to bring the destruction which exists in the planet in their home world to us to the federation and we tell you that this is essentially the most problematic that has come out of the situation right. What is what is this harm that we are talking about right. Given that we have established that these are not people who can sit with us on a table and decide right. Given that these are people who are essentially greedy people who are essentially aggressive people who have killed their own species to achieve certain means which are certain goals which they consider good. We tell you that it is a correct logical link to join the situation that once you bring them on the federation table once you make them aware of the resources that are available so far out in the galaxies once you make them aware that there is so much more you can be greedy for what they are essentially doing is inviting them to bring their destruction into our land. What you are essentially doing is giving them away an idea and opportunity to move out of the destruction limited to that one planet and bring it to so many other planets because there is so much more they can do there because they have not fucked that up yet because they can fuck that up yet and because there is so much benefit which they deem they can draw from this and because they are not capable of sitting it out on a trading table with you and I and discussing the benefit that they can achieve in this situation right. Having established this what have we told you so far in this debate and I believe you already understood it. On side of on side opening government this idea of pill is ridiculous. On side closing government this idea that like suddenly you are going to like control them means that they are already unfit and therefore Ira should not save them and hence we win that motion. But even if they were fit to save what are the harms and benefits that are lies over this the benefits is that suddenly we are going to like colonize them which we tell you is not the job of the Federation but the harms that arise of this situation is that they are going to bring discord in you like disharmony within the Federation and it says not only on the trading platform but they are also going to take their war and bring it to us and we tell you that is fundamentally not okay with us. So to save the Federation I propose that we vote for this motion before.

So let us locate some of the main clashes in the debate. The main clashes in the debate actually rested on the whole aspect of what are the possible benefits and what are the actual or speculative harms which are going to come out of it. So on the aspect of what are the actual benefits, right? So like the planet, like Ferdin has Ferdin being a separate planetary system who does have resources which can harness to our benefits. So if we were to have some support for space exploration programs, we believe that saving for them will further the cause of space exploration programs. We will save the cause of the space exploration programs and it's important to save and it's important to establish support for space exploration programs through such measures because if you want to show people that there are actual benefits, you are getting actual benefits through technological advancements, through resources, through other capabilities, through personnel, we need to actually, this also demands that we save for the, to garner support for space exploration programs within the Federation. Yeah, closing. This is because do you believe that the current situation that will be in existence, we also should save the people? Like I said, if you were to accept them within the Federation, we can actually bring them to one table and negotiate terms on our, negotiate terms on our, with according to our needs. So, why do we believe that side government and most particularly closing government with this debate is because we have come out and show you that, we have come out and show you that there are actual benefits occurring out of it. One is resources, second is support for space exploration programs, third is of creating the aggression of the residents of Erdil and all their loss and all the harms they have tried to put out are merely speculative and they do not consider the whole nature of what kind of negotiations we try, we intend to bring to the table and what, and negotiations to the point of even coercion where we give them the pill of survival in exchange for them to accept to our terms and hence we believe that their harms are neutralized under our model and for this reason we believe that we win this debate. Thank you.
judge_note: Persuasive on value imposition and resource benefits, with vivid nuclear analogy; slightly repetitive. Effective structure. Delivery steady but filler-heavy.
