And a country, a planet where the dominant species, right, are already there and they're trying to take away everything from those who are subservient, we tell you that this inherent characteristics are going to defeat the purpose of this organization, right? Because understand this, right? This is the point about space-sparing, right? The concept, the idea that today Fardin has space-sparing capabilities, right? We tell you that this greed is going to be taken elsewhere as well, right? Because understand this that as of now, Fardin does not know about this federation, right? And we tell you that the moment Fardin finds out about this federation and as a result of you are saving it, automatically become a part of this federation, we tell you that it is going to actually try to take over, you know, at least... And this is a perspective... If we are to look from the perspective of our own planet, we tell you that we face an inherent threat from these people and you know their entire ideology and having said that, we're very, very proud to...
judge_note: Convincing on unfit nature and destruction risks, with vivid self-interest examples; highly persuasive. Tight structure. Delivery fluent but less dynamic.

motion: THBT India should renounce their claim on Kashmir
speech_text: Mr. Speaker, we see that it is always good to run for peace for the international community to ensure that there is a peace on the planet. So we see that how this territory is built is actually causing a lot of tension between India and also Pakistan. We see that ever since before, there have been conflicts between Pakistan and India in terms of how they help Bangladesh achieve independence and also ever since World War II, we see that there have been a lot of these conflicts. And we see that a concession must be made to ensure that there will be peace. We see that we need to find an agreement for these two sides to ensure that as soon as possible, at the shortest time possible, to ensure that the rights of people in Kashmir will be taken care of. We see that right now in Kashmir, military atrocities have had awkward before. So we see that it's time for India to find a moral stance to resolve this issue. So we see that to remove this conflict in peace, we need to ensure that India should renounce the claim of Kashmir. So we see that how we should encourage these two countries to reach a consensus. We must ensure that India actually renounces this kind of claim to ensure that a decision can be made. For example, we can actually ensure that we should encourage Kashmir to find a proper ground for Kashmir to be in, which encourages people to organise a referendum of any other more peaceful way to solve this conflict. So we see that how we characterize renounce. We characterize renounce as we stop and end this kind of forceful claim by using government resources. So when we stop this kind of forceful claim, we encourage a peaceful decision to be made and we see that it should be other revenues to be explored to solve this conflict. So what we propose would be, we see that there are other peaceful ways to resolve this conflict, for example by referendum, by plebiscites and so on. And what not, we see this kind of, this kind of decision would be far better compared to this kind of forceful renounce, forceful claim on these parts, on Kashmir. We see that how it creates tension between two countries, this is really bad for the international community. Why should India renounce its claims when it has historical rights over the place? And as you said, there are other avenues to solve the tension. Okay, there are other avenues to solve this tension. How we must respect that the people in Kashmir, they have a bigger decision to make compared to India. These people, they have cultural ties to this place. So we see that the weight of these decisions should fall on the people of Kashmir. So on how we should not interfere with these people's rights to their own decisions. And these people, they can self-actualize that way, they can decide what they really want for their own future because essentially that's their future, that's their life, they are going to live as long as they live. So we see that these people should decide whether they want to go to India or Pakistan. So we see that referendums and plebiscites, it will be the best peaceful way to solve this conflict. Why man? Notice it now. So I'll tell you why it is important to renounce this claim and what change would actually occur if we encourage India to renounce this claim. So we see that it is really, really important because we need to ensure that this issue is resolved peacefully. But if we can actually ensure that these people of Kashmir, they will have closure, we will remove that kind of friction between these two countries. When we remove this kind of friction, we see that further economic and also foreign policy will be improved, how we improve the ties between these two countries, trades can occur, economy can be blue between these two countries. And we see that by resolving this conflict peacefully, we improve diplomatic ties and we see that it is still possible for these two countries to be good neighbours, they can help each other out if they reach this decision peacefully. So on the second hand on what change would we actually see, we see that it can actually save government resources on both sides of the country, for both India and also Pakistan on how we can ensure these two countries, they can move on, the people in Kashmir they can move on, they have proper closure, they can develop the parts of the countries that really need development, they do not need to invest emotionally, financially and whatnot in all these countries. Because essentially when we see that there is no guarantee to who this place actually belongs to, we see that the best possible way to solve this conflict quickly and as peaceful as possible would be through the referendum. And certainly I would like to talk about how it is important to respect the self-determination of the people of Kashmir, on how we should actually give these people the choice to make and how essentially this place, the rights of these people are not properly taken care of, because what the government really wants, what both sides of this country really wants is just that land and while they are still fighting for those lands, we see that these rights of the people are not properly taken care of because no government would want to invest their money on places that there is no proper guarantee for them. So we say that how these private sides will actually help these people because we allow them to determine their own fate and we allow these people to sit down, allow these people to see where they should go because these people they are in the best position to make this decision because they actually live in Kashmir and they know what is best for them. So even in the status quo, we see that in the case of LBA where there are different groups of people, Dinkangot and Mesurini, we see that these two groups of people, what is happening in LBA now is that the government is trying to allow these people to decide where they want to go through the referendum. We see that this is essentially good because it actually solves a lot of conflict. We see that how this is actually good because these people they are cultural ties to their own land. So we need to respect these kind of cultural ties, their living needs and whatnot. So we have to respect that you cannot displace these people because their livelihood depends on that place they have been living and they need to be, we need to respect that they should choose the government that suits them the best. So what is actually important is that we solve this conflict peacefully through referendum by ensuring for this referendum that actually we need to ensure that India renounce this kind of falsehood thing whereby we would allow other, more people who ever knew to be explored and ensure that the people of Kashmir deserve what they really should get. With this, we are proud to vote.
judge_note: Emphasizes peace and self-determination via referendum, with clear examples of India-Pakistan tensions; speculative on outcomes. Structure clear but repetitive. Delivery clear but filler-heavy, moderate energy.

motion: THBT India should renounce their claim on Kashmir
speech_text: Now Mr. Speaker, the stance on the opposition side is very clear. We believe that India should not renounce its claims on Kashmir because India has rights to claim for it and has historical facts to prove that it essentially has rights to prove that it belongs to them, right? Essentially, one of the major things that we need to understand is India is claiming over Kashmir because it has cultural, it has citizens of Indian descent over there, it has invested so much in all this stuff that I'll talk about. Pakistan is majorly claiming over Kashmir simply because India helped Bangladesh win the war and China is claiming because they want the resources. What am I going to talk to you today? I'm going to talk to you about how it is essentially India's right to seek the claim over Kashmir. Secondly, I'm going to talk about why India should not renounce their claim on Kashmir due to the fact that they have invested so much in an infrastructure over there. Thirdly, I'm going to talk to you about how renouncing their claim could essentially trigger a societal backlash within India and fourthly, I'm going to talk to you about why there needs to be a balance of interest in this particular situation. But before that, three rebuttals. Now they came up here and spoke about how we need to, we need India to renounce their claim because we need to promote peace. So, I mean, we need to understand that there has been a clash between the India and Pakistanis and we need to understand they have already established the fact that there are a lot of other avenues to solve these problems and we think that India renouncing it claims should not be one of the avenues. Why? Because India has right to claim over the place, something that I'll speak about in my point later. Secondly, they came up and talked about people's choice. But it's essentially like saying that if I have a child and someone else just decides to come and claim over my child and I should then leave the decision up to my child as to whether he wants to be their kid or my kid. This is a logical fantasy that is coming out of the government side because ultimately we believe referendums will not apply in this situation because when you talk about countries like China and Pakistan, they are willing to, their bigger picture is the resources over there and they are willing to spend millions to pay these people to then vote for them and things like this should not come into the context because when you look at a historical perspective, India has significant amount of influence and rights to seek claim over Kashmir. Thirdly, they came and talked about how this situation needs to happen because we need to save resources. Now I got two questions. I will reply to some two different levels. Number one, it is to say that just because you want to save resources, you don't want to seek claim over a land that is essentially yours. We think that this is undermining the value of Indian population that exists in Kashmir. And secondly, even if we assume, even if we buy that argument about resources, India essentially will lose resource if they renounce their claim because they are already extant, Mr. Speaker. Now I move on to my argument, four arguments. Number one, why it's legally to a certain extent, India's because during the day when India and Pakistan were together as one country, when they were on the verge of being split up, the king at that point of time, when he died, he passed India or he passed Kashmir over to India. It was a mistake by the British in stating their document. They didn't put it clearly and it mistakenly put Pakistan into the picture. So that is one of the major claims. And it is important to defend what is ultimately India because we believe that if India was, if Kashmir was significantly under what's given to India, then we think that India has the absolute moral need to defend whatever is there, something that I'll talk about in my further part. Secondly, also on the idea of infrastructure. Now if we look at Kashmir as a whole, there has been a lot of conflicts happening over there, but ultimately a lot, a large junk of the infrastructure that has been built for people over there has been coming from Indian government. We have invested in the Indian government has invested significant amount of money over there to cater. Why do they invest significant amount of money? Because first there are people of Indian descent over there, Indian people with relatives that stay in Kashmir. So this is one of the reasons why they go on to invest in those places. And we think that it will be a bit unlogical for them to simply pull out when the fact that is that they have spent so much of money in that place in the first place. Thirdly, we also think that there's this idea of societal backlash. Now when we talk about a country and its power, it's derived from the fact that the country has ability to show what it is, what belongs to it and what does not belong to it. In terms of Kashmir, if India really believes that it has legal rights to claim over it, then it should claim because it could trigger societal backlash in the form of, in terms of the Indian population will not like it. Number one, because even in Kashmir, there is a huge amount of Indian population over there. So to essentially pull out, we'll send a message to the public in India that we are bailing out of these people who are hoping on us to continuously fight for them and make Kashmir Indians. We think that is one of the major problems, one of the major problems that we go through. Secondly, we also need to cater for this Indians in that are in Kashmir. It is a bit unfair just because the places were split and just because a lot of people are seeking claims over it. It is a bit unfair for you to just let go, you know what, we don't want more, so we are fine, you just go, you just go under them. It is something that we should not do, especially to Indian citizens who are in Kashmir. And thirdly, it hurts the legitimacy of the country as a whole. It looks that in just because India is not wanting conflict, it is willing to give up its rights on Kashmir. Yes. You've talked about everything and everyone, but Kashmir, tell us why if Kashmir people want autonomy and sovereignty, why can't they get it? Because the Indian, because the Indian country has rights historically over that place. And there are people in that place that want to be part of India. And we think it is a bit unfair to hurt these minorities. Yes, what if a small group of minorities still wants to be under India? And we think we still need to respect their decisions as well. Their opinions do matter and that is one of the main reasons why we think India should stay. And but also, fourthly, there is this idea of balance of interest because India has oil rigs over there and we all know the reason why China wants to come into the picture is because they want to take the resources out of the region. And we think that if India pulls out of it, there will be a shift in balance. Number one, pulling out of Kashmir could hurt India economically because of the oil rigs and the resources that come from Kashmir, pushes India's economy to a certain extent. And we think that pulling out of it could hurt India economically. But secondly, also, it would allow the other states like Pakistan to capitalize on this infrastructure that India has built over the years in terms of the oil rigs, in terms of the basic infrastructure. It would allow the countries like Pakistan and China who naturally want control over that place to then take over. And we think that it's naturally unfair. So what is opening up? What is opening opposition brought to you today? We have spoke to you about why it's to a certain extent it's legally India's right to seek claim on it. We have spoken to you about why India has invested so much in it and why that should be a reason as to why India should not renounce its claims. And thirdly, we have spoken to you how it could trigger societal backlash. And fourthly, we have proven to you why there is a balance of interest that needs to be maintained. With that, we were very proud to oppose. Thank you.
judge_note: Strong on historical rights and infrastructure investment, with vivid examples; slightly overstated on societal backlash. Well-organized, minor tangents. Delivery energetic but filler-heavy.

motion: THBT India should renounce their claim on Kashmir
speech_text: Four issues of address in this debate. First, why something that occurs in the past has less varying on the decision that we make today? Second, whether the resources are justified, we spent. Third, where does the interest of India lie and lastly on why is our policy better? Why is self-determination better for Kashmir? We're going to the first issue on why something that occurs in the past has less varying on what the decisions that we make today. Because we tell you that over the past, although sometimes we cannot deny that some certain historical events have occurred in the past and there are a lot of things that there are a lot of claims that India can make over Kashmir. But at the end of the day, the calculus has changed over the years. There have been three wars between India and Pakistan on claiming Kashmir and we think that this has changed the calculus and the how fragile the situation is. Because we tell you that once we were looking at a situation right now, Charlene told you that peace is the most important goal at the end of the day. Why? Because we tell you that when peace is achieved and when unresolved issues are deadlocked, they are actually resolved, both parties or all parties in the issue will benefit from this because there are certain benefits that they can reap from diplomatic ties or from the determination of Kashmir as a country on its own or who owns the territory and these kinds of things will eventually bring to more benefits to all parties in the situation. I'll elaborate more on this a bit later. So the only harm they said, they pursued that will happen under our parents was this. The countries that are richer will want to pay their citizens to vote for them in their referendum. Firstly, we don't think that this will really happen because regulations can be done, can actually deter this and we say that secondly, when people want to determine where, what is the status of their future, we don't think that money is the first thing that they take into consideration. We don't think that the people of Kashmir will want to identify with a country, identify as a citizen of a country that they cannot relate to culturally or they do not have family members or they will have a lot of other things that they would want to factor in in making this decision. We don't think that this harm that side-opportunity will happen will actually occur under when we hold a referendum. But on the idea that they said that rich houses are justifiably spent because there are certain people, there are certain citizens of India, the people of India in Kashmir which we need to protect. But we think that at the end of the day, there must be a cost-benefit analysis of what is the benefit of actually spending your money, your budget into resolving this issue which is continuously going on. And we don't think that it will lead anywhere and no one wants to make a move and want to give in and give up certain rights to ensure a better future. So we think that this has been, at the end of the day, when we talk about resources, we think that the government of India has lost more than what they spent and we think that they should stop channeling their resources in reclaiming Kashmir as it is. So where does the interest of India lie? We tell you when peace settlements happen, when a peaceful settlement happens, even if they have to give up Kashmir, if they have, if they, there are two scenarios, whether they get Kashmir or they do get Kashmir, Kashmir, so determines themselves as a country of its own, with their own sovereign borders or whatever their referendum result would be. But we think that the trade-off is this, peace versus historical rights that they want to claim. We think that the investment that they invested in Kashmir has already, the efforts that India has made to actually reclaim or to claim Kashmir as theirs has already exceeded the investment that they made and if this goes on, we think that it will cost more and more and we don't think that the investment is something which should take into consideration. But at the end of the day, when we talk, the second thing that they talked about was their citizens in Kashmir that India has to protect and has to fight for so that Kashmir can be annexed into India. But we don't think that this is the case. When we get some determination rights to them, at the end of the day, these people will still be protected because their rights will still be, they will still have a right to vote and they will have this room for them to have a say in what their future and the Government of India is not necessarily violating any of their rights as a citizen, if they were citizens before or later. What about the people in case of your referendum and there are people who choose to be part of India? What about these people then? The window there minorities? When we talk about international relations, that is the balance that we have to make because they will be people, they will always be people who lose out and people who gain and we think that this is what we can debate on is whether or not this is the best decision to make at the end of the day. Why is our policy better? Because we take self-determination ensures a few things, ensures that the policy is resolved, ensures that long-term decisions can be made for the state of Kashmir, for the future of Kashmir. We tell that if India continues to assert this claim on Kashmir, what will happen? It will just be a continuous form of deadlock and there will be no form of, there will be no party in this conflict wanting to resolve the issue and this is particularly harmful. We tell you that once the issue is resolved, everyone can forget about what happened in Kashmir, in the conflicts that happened in the past and they can start moving forward and starting to form diplomatic ties, starting to start engaging in trade and other forms of international relations. We think that this is better for everyone in this conflict. So I'm going to extend what Shalini told you by talking about this, how we open doors, if they renounce their claim on Kashmir, it's going to open doors for other conflicts in the future to happen because without a proper resolution, there are a few scenarios that can happen because in the past we tell you that there are a few wars which already broke out and already happened one after another and a lot of casualties have happened in these fours and a lot of people have died in the process, a lot of money has been spent in the process. We think that there's a high likelihood for another war to happen if another conflict with Pakistan happens in the future. Why? Because there's history of India and Pakistan is a very delicate one, right? Because there were a lot of ways where there was a war between India and Pakistan that happened after the World War II which got international attention because a lot of people, because India had entered the war and it was Pakistan and this conflict between them shows that the relationship between them is very delicate. So we think that there are other potential conflicts that will happen when a territory is not determined as who it belongs to or whether they're a self-sovereign country. Because at the end of the day, 20 years down the line, people, individuals will start, probabilities that individuals will take up small scales, they will want to resort to self-initiated actions to actually reclaim the lands, such as what happened in Lahat Datu when the people of Sulu, they claimed that the land was theirs. The issue must be resolved now and must be resolved with the most cortacious manner. We think that what Shalini told you in this debate was very important. We told you that peace was what is most important for this debate. The best interests of India and all other parties in this debate is protected. Under the side of OG, where we tell you that a referendum must be held, the other side is the way to go. We propose.
judge_note: Reinforces peace and self-determination, with clear war history examples; repetitive on benefits. Clear structure, minor tangents. Delivery steady but filler-heavy, moderate energy.

motion: THBT India should renounce their claim on Kashmir
speech_text: Ms. Speaker, so we believe that this is extremely inconvenient for the opening government to believe that peace is ultimately going to come through the element of a referendum and everything is going to be solved through the minority rights being acknowledged. We believe this is extremely delusional for a number of reasons, which we'll state to you later. Now, first of all, the substantive I'm going to bring about, the first, that any retraction of a claim towards India is a threat to India's national security. Secondly, there is an international impact, well, there is an impact on the international community in terms of the drug problem. And those two are my substantive. Before I actually get into that, there are a number of root buttocks that I want to get in. Now, first of all, they seem to believe that, okay, now, first of all, they brought out the point that things that happened in the past should not be character to the fact that conflicts are going on right now. And at the moment, essential lives are being lost, people, harms are being committed, etc., But the bottom line we'd like to bring out here is that when you're arguing about the geographical borders of a country, that conflict is about to go on for very long because the argument is essentially about the resources within those geographical borders, which each country is not willing to split up. Now, first of all, let me give you an example of Israel and Palestine. At the moment, they're going amok about the fact that they don't know which part is Israel, which part is Palestine, and one is obviously taking over the other. Now, we've already showed to you in the substantive support by my partner that the situation is delicate because of the tri-state-like situation over Kashmir. There's a balance of interest currently between India, Pakistan, and China in order to keep things at a stable region because we believe at the moment, out of the constitutional theory, that Kashmir does not have the legal precedents to be able to become a country, nor the economic precedents to do so. Now, secondly, self-determination is not going to be a right given to any minority just because he wants to get independence. Any example of that, Catalonia in Spain is always going to be kept at a higher and arms behest because the fact that the state government, federal government, will not allow for a referendum to even be recognized, and that is the prerogative of the federal government because of the fact that it is definitely Indian territory. Now, we've established to you earlier, it is Indian territory. It will always be Indian territory. It is legally ours. There is no changing that. We've already invested infrastructure into it. There are a stable number of populations that we are supposed to be protecting. The fact that their economy, their basic economy is actually the social security patterns in Jammu and Kashmir are being sustained by the Indian government, and not a single cent is being spent by Pakistan and China. It just goes to show that if India would even retract its claims, there will be people that are going to be, that are going to be suffering from an obvious power vacuum. Go ahead. How can historical rights shock over self-determination of these people? Because these people, the future of these people are at stake, essentially, not historical rights. Keele, let me give you a little example. The British fought for the Falkland Islands for a very long time because they had colonized the entire situation. They stated that it was British land and they were going to go and protect it as much as they wanted. Because they recognized the fact that it was theirs and even though lives were lost, they still were willing to maintain the sovereignty of that land. We believe that India has the relevant right to do so. Thank you. Secondly, the idea that a simple generational challenge, sorry, a shift in viewpoints of a new generation just because the past should not determine the current generation is actually futile, actually is nonsensical because the fact that there are already extremist factions within the Pakistani-controlled Kashmiri population that are posing a national security challenge, I'll be talking about a little later. I mean, these people are already catering to the menaces of society that are affecting most of Southeast Asia anyway. But before I get into that, you also believe that India has lost resources. Well, guess what? We've stated earlier that if we have potential resource factors, we have, the fact is, Kashmir is so beautiful that everyone is ready to go to war for it. India has shot Bollywood films there, which is their major source of income. They have oil rigs. They have so many things to capitalize from. They will not let it go. And just because a dissenting minority who obviously do not have the economic or the political prowess to take care of themselves on the constitutional record, the constitutional theory of recognizing what is a country, India shouldn't, if they choose to follow the fringes for not conforming to the majority. Then so be it. The bottom line is the majority feels, the majority of the Indian population feels that Kashmir is theirs and we're going to keep to the public mandate of India. Lastly, the efficacy of referendums in the first place, because they believe that referendums of lebiscites are going to be the big magic wand that's pretty much going to solve everything we believe this is out of bullshit. Because of the fact that we've seen Somali land in Somalia and the fact that in 1925 when the Somali land, people actually came up with a referendum. Even the United Nations were not able to recognize it because of the sheer fact that referendums cannot be conducted holistically because of the lack of a census. And if Damur and Kashmir is such a much such a mountainous region that even an efficient census cannot be conducted, then what so is a referendum? And obviously you do not understand these politics when you don't realize the fact that the influencing factors of power within the region, such as the Pakistanis and the Indians, not willing to not just use bribery in order to consort votes towards reference, but intimidation as well, which is Indian politics and Pakistani politics. One or one is your your idea of being delusional of what the reality of the ground might be. First of all, now I shall move into my second piece, which is the national security of India. Now, first of all, there is already a number of terrorist factions that are actually being created within the India, within the Kashmiri region. Some of them are even responsible for the Mumbai touch bombings that that had happened. And you can actually trace them to Kashmiri territory. And secondly, a lot of the a lot of the general population that actually want independent, so to speak, are actually trying to retaliate towards Indian land claiming. But and the fact of the matter is they're extremists because they're willing to go towards the most extreme forms of dissent to be able to prove a point. Bottom line is they can't come to the table and play fair. We're not going to let you go for it. Secondly, there is an international impact towards the Kashmir in situation. Now, obviously, if you study your map, the Kashmir is actually on the opium route trade and is also one of the places where you actually is actually physically located on a critical area where drugs are being actually trapped throughout the Southeast Asian region. Now, India is already taking its efforts to the extreme to be able to police the situation. If India loses any form of control, it loses traction on the fight against drugs, which in turn affects the international community. Do we want a politically and economically defunct people with no sense of rationality? And the only thing they want is independence because it's something to be fed to them by an opposing country who is willing to take their resources from underneath them. I don't think so. The bottom line is we are worse.
judge_note: Compelling on national security and drug trade risks, with vivid examples; overstates referendum flaws. Tight structure. Delivery fluent but filler-heavy.

motion: THBT India should renounce their claim on Kashmir
speech_text: They told us that a child can't choose who their parents are because of DNA. And they use the same circular line of reasoning to explain why India should not renounce because it's India's territory, therefore they should not renounce it. I'll explain to you why, even all their reasons that they've given us to explain why Kashmir is India's territory is not applicable and why even though OG did not fill the gaps, if you pass the referendum, there will be an overwhelming majority that will agree that Kashmir should be a sovereign state of its own and therefore that's the assumption that CG is going to run its arguments on. Let's look at what they've given us as reasons for why India, Kashmir is India's. Firstly, they told us India is technically legally there's because of, sorry, Kashmir is legally there's because of historical reasons. We don't agree with this like colonialistic rhetoric, especially when it comes to seeing how countries can even attain autonomy post historical events. And we don't believe that this is the one reason or the one main factor why it should even be considered should be considered in assessing Kashmir's sovereignty. Why is this so? This is because we recognize that Kashmir, even though it may be in India claims it is a limb of India, it is a defective limb and it has been diverging away from India. The people, the culture in Kashmir itself has been independent of India, has been growing independently from India itself and we don't believe that historical reasons should cling to people who are completely opposite to one like one singular constitution. Second, they told us like that that India invested infrastructure. It's a complete childish reason to use to say that like because you invested infrastructure you have your mind now. Because there's a simple solution to that right, you can always have like offer Kashmir a form of loan or like Kashmir can always pay it back in return. But more than that, we don't believe that this is a fundamental reason that you should use to like deny a birth of a sovereign nation in and of itself. So we don't agree that that's good enough. Third, they told us about societal backlash like Indians are going to get angry about that. First of all, when they conceded that it was a it was predominantly historical reasons and after 60 years we know that the modern common Indian man does not really care about what happens to Kashmir anymore because it was no because we've already seen this happen like even okay on the most like in the most obvious of instances where you see like clashes between India and Pakistan on cricket fields and like you know the fans and all that it's becoming less about the concept it's becoming less politicized as compared to before and we believe that as the generations go on the Indian the modern Indian person will not care but even if that's not the case we don't believe or we don't agree why the Indian man's like outrage is more important than the Kashmiri like suffering in oppression or in like in fear of not having its own sovereign nation. The last reason they talked about is that it's going to hurt India's legitimacy. I'm going to give you my extension to explain why it will not hurt India's legitimacy but rather be something that India would want to embrace. Then the second speaker came up to us and told us about how it's going to be a threat to national security because of terrorists and stuff like that. Transition comes at a cost and we agree that there will be a power vacuum that has to be filled but we really agree we already see the trend towards democracy setting place even in like the in the 1940s in Kashmir itself right that's why you have no thank you you had people who keep coming up with revolutions to try to make Kashmir independent and we say that as it is no longer like the democracy is no longer nascent to Kashmir anymore. The last idea on drug fights I don't know how it's relevant but you can always solve it with a transnational issue so we don't we don't believe it's like important in any way. Now let me move on to my arguments. Firstly we have to explain I'm going to explain to you why Kashmir is catered on the path towards sovereignty and autonomy.
